charts.title: Actively queried counterexamples close the hidden-failure gap (ceiling split)
charts.note: Visible-only selection passes 77.5% hidden; actively chosen counterexamples reach 97.5% at +12 queries, beating random at every budget.

charts.title: Selection policy comparison at a 6-query budget, across splits
charts.note: Six actively chosen cases recover most of the oracle's gain on all three splits; random queries at the same budget lag well behind.

charts.title: Support split: active selection reaches 100% hidden full-pass at +12 queries
charts.note: The hardest split for visible-only selection (70%) is fully solved by active querying at +12, matching the oracle upper bound.

charts.title: Accuracy vs probes spent: SFT stop/more matches full budget at half the cost
charts.note: The SFT controller hits fixed-budget-10 accuracy (92.5%) with 4.81 probes, near the oracle's 4.22 — above the fixed-budget Pareto curve.

charts.title: Hidden-all accuracy by stopping policy
charts.note: The base model stops almost immediately (5% accuracy); SFT lifts the same model to 92.5%, tying oracle stopping and the full 10-probe budget.

charts.title: Adaptive spend: probes used per task template
charts.note: The SFT policy spends few probes on easy affine-mod tasks and more on hard compare-gate tasks, tracking the oracle while matching its accuracy.

charts.title: Held-out accuracy: learned controller vs direct answering and diagnostics
charts.note: A one-feature learned controller lifts accuracy 42% to 50% cheaply, but a fixed shape rule reaches the 58% oracle ceiling.

charts.title: Accuracy vs token cost: the controller is a low-cost Pareto point
charts.note: Direct-only, learned controller, and the fixed shape rule form the deployable frontier; the full-budget oracle costs ~4x more for the same 58%.

charts.title: Where the controller helps: per-family accuracy, direct vs controller
charts.note: All controller gains come from two structural-split families; it never hurts families the direct answer already handles.

charts.title: Held-out frontier accuracy: plain bridge data beats discriminative selection
charts.note: Equal-family bridge records lift hard-frontier accuracy from 60% to 99%; hard-case discriminative selection only hurts.

charts.title: Trace controls: the bridge adapter really reads the execution trace
charts.note: Removing traces costs 8 points but shuffling them collapses accuracy to 16%, so the model is semantically dependent on trace content.

charts.title: Where discriminative selection lost ground (hard frontier, by family)
charts.note: Hard-case selection trades gains on a few families for large regressions on others; the plain static bridge stays near-perfect everywhere.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge_report.md, Hard Frontier By Family)

charts.title: Budget-3 hidden-all accuracy: SFT bucket ranker barely beats split top-1
charts.note: The SFT ranker captures 1.25 of the 12.5-point oracle gap over split top-1; most target-aware headroom stays unconverted.

charts.title: Budget-3 accuracy by task template
charts.note: Affine-mod is nearly solved by every arm; compare-gate is where the oracle gap lives, and the SFT ranker recovers almost none of it.

charts.title: Frozen-ABI oracle coverage collapses on held-out tasks (Gate 1 failed)
charts.note: Coverage drops from 83.8% (calibration) to 13.8% (held-out) while false passes soar, so compiler training was not run.

charts.title: Coverage collapse holds across every task category
charts.note: No category is spared: even dict/counter tasks fall from 100% covered on calibration to 14% on held-out tasks.

charts.title: Random held-out resamples confirm the fixed slice was not unlucky
charts.note: Three random held-out subsets stay at 17.5-19.4% coverage (mean 18.3%), close to the fixed slice's 13.8%.

charts.title: ABI oracle coverage climbs the rung ladder, first-visible selection lags

series.label: oracle coverage (any candidate passes all tests)

charts.note: The final reusable ABI can express 134/160 tasks, but naive first-visible selection only gets 95/160 — a coverage gate, not a solver.

charts.title: Final-rung coverage by task slice
charts.note: Coverage stays above 79% in every classified slice except the catch-all 'other' bucket, where both coverage and selection drop.

charts.title: Task-level false-visible-pass rate falls as the ABI grows

series.label: false visible pass (among tasks with a visible-consistent candidate)

charts.y_label: Tasks fooled by the one visible test (%)
charts.note: Richer ABIs cut single-visible-test false passes from 58% to 9%, but per-candidate hidden-wrong pressure stays near 80% — verification is still required.

charts.title: Most covered tasks need only a depth-1 ABI program
charts.note: 94 of 134 covered tasks are solved by a single primitive; genuine composition (depth 2-3) accounts for 40 tasks.

charts.title: Coverage and pass@1 on held-out MBPP: constrained DPO vs base sampling
charts.note: Constrained DPO at K=4 beats base K=4 and the shuffled control without hurting pass@1, but stays below the K=8 sample-more reference.

charts.title: Token cost of each arm: coverage gains vs forward tokens spent
charts.note: Constrained DPO reaches 62.5% coverage at roughly half the tokens of K=8 sampling (66.7%), a better token point but not a coverage win.
charts.source: reports/report_summary.json (eval arms, token_usage.forward_tokens), also report table

charts.title: Training loss over 10 optimizer steps: real vs shuffled preference pairs
charts.note: Both LoRA runs were deliberately stopped at 10 steps; the constrained objective keeps loss near the DPO floor rather than collapsing.

charts.title: Counterexample-trained traces help greedy repair but not reranking
charts.note: On counterexample traces, training on them lifts greedy repair 51.4% to 58.3% but slightly hurts rerank-selected accuracy (63.9% to 61.1%).

charts.title: Effect is family-specific: rescue on two families, total collapse on one
charts.note: Counterexample training reaches 100% on modulo and 75% on tuple but collapses to 0% on the length family via a stable count_eq alias.

charts.title: Coherent traces matter: shuffled traces are worse than no traces
charts.note: The adapter reads traces rather than ignoring them: removing traces costs 5.5pp, but corrupting them cuts accuracy from 58.3% to 22.2%.

charts.title: Depth-3 solve rate: one-shot sampling vs decompose-and-compose search
charts.note: Decompose-and-compose search cracks depth-3 at 3.2-3.4x the monolithic rate, but brute force matches guided search: the crack is structure + interpreter.

charts.title: Solve rate vs interpreter-call budget: guidance buys efficiency, not coverage
charts.note: Model guidance wins at low budgets but plateaus (the planner-wall); brute enumeration keeps climbing and matches or beats it at high budget.

charts.title: Banking search-found solutions lifts the model's own one-shot sampling (held-out, n=80)
charts.note: QLoRA-SFT on 327 search-harvested solutions (no teacher) lifts pass@5 +0.11 and depth-3 pass@5 4x — the frontier extension M4 could not achieve.

charts.title: Retro-audit: how much of the depth-3 crack is truly depth-3?
charts.note: 40% of nominal depth-3 tasks collapse to depth <=2; decompose solves all of those but only 17% of true depth-3 (monolithic: 0/24).

charts.title: Compare-gate: deployable greedy probing vs target-aware oracle
charts.note: More budget or more visible observations closes most of the 70-point oracle gap: the bottleneck is information volume, not policy.

charts.title: Compare-gate accuracy vs probe budget (4 visible observations)
charts.note: In the hardest regime greedy climbs steadily with budget, from 0% to 86%; the oracle saturates by budget 5.

charts.title: Compare-gate accuracy vs visible observations (budget 3)
charts.note: Holding budget at 3, each extra batch of visible observations lifts greedy sharply and shrinks the oracle gap from 70 to 6 points.

charts.title: Told the pipeline, the 4B executes near-perfectly; left to identify it, it fails
charts.note: The compositional wall is 100% identification, 0% execution: plan-given stays 0.90-1.00 through depth 4; seeing intermediates barely helps.

charts.title: Solve rate collapses ~30x in odds per composed op, regardless of op type
charts.note: On collapse-free tasks the wall is at depth 2, not 3, and information-destroying ops barely matter — refuting the destruction hypothesis.

charts.title: Even pre-segmented steps and yes/no verification decay with depth
charts.note: The deficit is multi-step mental simulation: isolated depth-1 identification runs at 0.88, yet composed contexts drag it down and verification nears chance (0.5).

charts.title: The old depth-3 wall was mismeasured: decompose search only worked on collapsed tasks
charts.note: 40% of nominal depth-3 tasks were behaviorally shallower; every recorded monolithic depth-3 solve rode on them — true depth-3 monolithic solves were 0.

charts.title: Hidden-test coverage: diverse sampling recovers most base misses
charts.note: More and more-diverse sampling lifts coverage from 70% to 88.75%; visible-test repair adds nothing over the union.

charts.title: Base-miss recovery: complementary decoding policies stack
charts.note: The union of three decoding policies recovers 13/24 misses vs 10 for the best single arm - the policies recover different tasks.

charts.title: Coverage vs forward-token budget
charts.note: Coverage keeps rising with sampling budget but with diminishing returns; this no-training curve is the bar any adapter must beat.

charts.title: Held-out hidden-case success by adapter and prompt condition
charts.note: Executable-program posttraining transfers to held-out families (75%), but only when the prompt carries aligned visible traces.

charts.title: Per-family held-out success: initial vs conjunction-support adapter
charts.note: The 0/24 failure on length+contains was a specific mechanism error; adding training-only conjunction families lifted it to 7/24 without hurting the others.

charts.title: Trace ablation: per-family hidden success for the conjunction-support adapter
charts.note: Aligned visible traces do real work: removing or shuffling them collapses tuple_branch_label from 95.8% to at most 20.8%.

charts.title: Adaptive shape router matches the oracle ceiling at a fraction of the fixed portfolio's cost
charts.note: The pilot-selected router (run programs when direct output loses columns) hits the 58% oracle ceiling; always-run-everything gets 56%.

charts.title: Token cost per task for the same policies
charts.note: The adaptive router spends about half the tokens of the fixed portfolio while beating its accuracy; the oracle shows further headroom.

charts.title: Fixed program budgets: accuracy saturates below the adaptive router
charts.x_label: Number of program strategies always run
charts.note: Spending the whole 5-program budget on every task never reaches the 58% the adaptive router gets by spending it selectively.

charts.title: Where the router's gains come from: per-family test accuracy
charts.note: All eight recoveries are concentrated in two families (merge/split and synthetic_8); the router never hurts a family the direct answer already solves.

charts.title: Paying for one program strategy first (canary) barely beats running it outright
charts.note: Canary escalation adds at most 2pp over the lone strategy and every variant stays below the shape router's 58%.

charts.title: Requiring consensus among program candidates hurts accuracy at every budget
charts.note: Demanding agreement between program outputs drops accuracy by 8-12pp versus committing to the first visible-consistent program.

charts.title: Looser shape triggers reach the same 29/50 accuracy at up to 3.5x the token bill
charts.note: The five selective triggers all score 29/50; only 'always' slips to 28/50 while paying the full 890k-token portfolio cost.

charts.title: Foofah held-out exact accuracy: direct generation vs frozen ABI
charts.note: Directly asking the 4B model to emit the transformed table (55.2%) beats the ABI/compiler route (18%); ABI adds only a small fallback slice.

charts.title: Who solves which cases: overlap of direct generation and ABI (250 cases)
charts.note: Direct generation uniquely solves 103 cases; the ABI's unique contribution is just 10, making it a fallback rather than the main route.

charts.title: Held-out exact-match rate on Foofah by strategy (n=250)
charts.note: Ephemeral program induction is far weaker than direct output; even a perfect selector would add only 4pp over direct.

charts.title: Where direct output and induced programs succeed (250 cases)
charts.note: Programs recover only 10 cases direct generation misses, while direct alone solves 110 that programs miss.

charts.title: Program-induction pipeline attrition (250 cases)
charts.note: The bottleneck is passing the visible example (47/250), and 9 of those 47 visible-pass programs are still wrong on held-out data.

charts.title: Prompt-iteration smokes before the full run
charts.note: Easy prefix cases flattered the program arm; on the harder spread it collapsed to 12%, and 'context' kept the only direct-failure recovery.

charts.title: Frozen transformation ABI on external Foofah benchmark (250 cases)
charts.note: The frozen ABI solves only 18% of held-out cases; selection (17.2%) nearly matches the oracle, so expressivity, not selection, is the bottleneck.

charts.title: Coverage vs number of example pairs shown
charts.note: Held-out coverage is flat from 1 to 5 examples: failures come from missing primitives, not ambiguity; extra examples only prune coincidence fits.

charts.title: Benchmark families: all-or-nothing coverage
charts.note: Coverage is all-or-nothing by family: 9 fold/unpivot/extract families are fully solved, the other 41 of 50 get zero held-out coverage.

charts.title: Held-out exact-match accuracy by selection policy (250 Foofah tasks)
charts.note: The simple first-visible-passing selector beats consensus and reaches 52%, capturing most of the 54% oracle union over direct JSON's 44.4%.

charts.title: Recoveries vs losses relative to direct JSON, per selector
charts.note: Consensus never loses a direct-correct case but recovers few misses; first-visible recovers 23 misses at the cost of only 4 losses.

charts.title: Program-commit precision by selector
charts.note: Stricter consensus makes each committed program more likely correct, but the precision gain is too small to offset its far lower coverage.

charts.title: Visible-example passes per prompt variant: initial vs repair-added
charts.note: One visible-feedback repair round nearly doubles structural_python's passes but adds little to the other variants.

charts.title: Held-out exact-match accuracy by arm (Foofah, n=250)
charts.note: Repair lifts program accuracy from 16% to 24.8%, and the program path adds +18 cases over direct alone (oracle union 62.4%).

charts.title: Repair progress by round (cases out of 250)
charts.note: Each repair round converts more failed programs, with diminishing returns: 40 correct at round 0, then +16, +5, +1.
charts.source: reports/report.md (Repair By Round table) and reports/eval_summary.json round_stats

charts.title: Verification risk: visible-example pass vs held-out truth
charts.note: About one fifth (20.5%) of programs that pass the visible example are still wrong on the held-out table, so naive commit-on-pass is unsafe.

charts.title: Prompt iteration on the 25-case smoke spread: standard vs strict repair prompt
charts.note: Explicitly warning the model against hardcoding visible outputs doubled final program correctness and cut the false-pass rate.

charts.title: Held-out Foofah accuracy: direct JSON vs frozen strategy portfolio
charts.note: The dev-selected consensus_2 policy gains modestly (42%→48%); the predeclared first-visible selector nearly reaches the 58% oracle.

charts.title: Program-commit precision vs recoveries by selector (held-out)
charts.note: First-visible commits more programs and recovers 8 direct misses at only 1 loss; consensus rules are safer but too conservative.

charts.title: Token cost of the strategy portfolio vs direct generation (held-out run)
charts.note: The portfolio spends about 12x more tokens than direct generation, so the accuracy gain is not yet an efficient policy.

charts.title: Held-out Foofah accuracy by fallback policy
charts.note: The simplest rule wins: committing the program whenever it passes the visible example gains +7.2pp over direct JSON, with zero losses.

charts.title: When program and direct answer disagree, who is right? (26 cases)
charts.note: On all 26 visible-disagreement cases the direct answer was never correct, while the program was correct 18 times.

charts.title: Program commits vs correct commits per policy
charts.note: Probe-thresholded policies commit few programs and reject useful wins; committing on visible pass keeps ~80% precision at full volume.

charts.title: Probe agreement does not separate correct from wrong programs
charts.note: Counterexample probes were an anti-signal: wrong programs had slightly higher agreement with independent direct answers than correct ones.

charts.title: Held-out exact-match accuracy: discovered strategies vs baselines (50 Foofah tasks)
charts.note: Discovered strategies beat direct JSON by up to 4pp but add zero tasks beyond the included baseline pool (56-58%).

charts.title: Where policies diverge: per-family accuracy on the three differentiating families
charts.note: Discovered strategies recover merge_split tasks that direct generation misses, but the baseline pool already solves those plus synthetic_8.

charts.title: Quality of the two discovered strategy cards across all 50 held-out tasks
charts.note: Each discovered strategy solves only 6-10 of 50 tasks on its own; their value is narrow, family-specific coverage.

charts.title: Best-of-8 accuracy: the model's own verifier closes most of the oracle gap
charts.note: A zero-training thinking self-verifier picks best-of-8 at 86%, closing 75% of the pass@1-to-oracle gap (24% without thinking).

charts.title: Intrinsic self-verification skill: thinking turns the model into a real critic
charts.note: Thinking adds +0.20 balanced accuracy and +0.15 AUROC when judging the model's own candidates correct vs incorrect.

charts.title: Yes-bias control: without thinking the verifier mostly agrees with the generator
charts.note: No-think says 'correct' 91% of the time vs a true 77% pass rate; thinking lowers the yes-bias toward the truth.

charts.title: Held-out ceiling: GraphIR pipeline does not beat the DSL baseline
charts.note: Repair lifts GraphIR from 21.7% to 24.2%, but the plain DSL baseline still wins at 29.2% (out of 120 tasks).

charts.title: Repair skill exists: synthetic corrupted graphs before vs after repair
charts.note: On synthetic corrupted GraphIR, repair jumps 3.3% to 26.7% — the skill is real but does not transfer to the model's own construction errors.

charts.title: Ceiling families where representations diverge most
charts.note: Gains and losses are family-specific: GraphIR unlocks one family DSL fails (83.3% vs 0%) but collapses on others DSL solves.

charts.title: Every stopping policy selects the hidden-correct candidate at the same 16.7% rate
charts.note: A correct candidate exists for every task (100% coverage), yet no probe-spending policy lifts selection above 16.7% — a clean negative.

charts.title: Executable probes spent per task by stopping policy
charts.note: The SFT controller learned to stop cheaper than fixed budget 8 (3.83 vs 8 probes), but since accuracy is flat the saving buys nothing.

charts.title: Frozen ABI oracle coverage collapses on held-out tasks
charts.note: The frozen ABI covers 37.5% of calibration tasks but only 14.4% held-out, failing the gate for a compiler-training run.

charts.title: Held-out coverage is stable across three random-sample seeds
charts.note: Three-seed sweep averages 18.3% (range 18.1-18.8%), confirming low held-out coverage is not a sampling fluke.

charts.title: Held-out coverage is low in every task slice
charts.note: No slice exceeds 21% held-out coverage; numeric tasks are worst at 4.4%, so the gap is not confined to one domain.

charts.title: Visible-test filtering is unreliable: false-pass pressure by split
charts.note: On held-out tasks 59.6% of visible passes have no full winner and 91.9% of visible-consistent candidates fail hidden tests.

charts.title: Consensus selection outcomes on 24 residual MBPP tasks
charts.note: Strict independent consensus never committed (0/24); same-neighborhood consensus committed rarely and mostly on wrong answers.

charts.title: Pool quality: coverage vs false-pass risk by retrieval pool
charts.note: Diversified independent retrieval lowered coverage below the same-neighborhood control and raised the visible-pass false-positive rate.

charts.title: Two-hole shortlist coverage: a null result across all controls
charts.note: QLoRA training did not help: base, trained, and shuffled-inventory controls all miss the exact 1024-candidate budget (16 records each).

charts.title: Designed six-query probes shrink ambiguity far more than random cases
charts.note: Max-split query design cuts the average consistent candidate set from ~22.4k to ~5.7k on low-information comparison records.

charts.title: Designed probes also pick the right program more often

series.label: selected program passes all hidden cases

charts.note: Designed probes lift hidden-case success from 28.6% to 42.9% - helpful, but not enough to solve selection alone.

charts.title: QLoRA training loss over 80 steps
charts.note: Loss drops quickly then plateaus around 1.6, so the null eval result is not a failure to fit the training format.

charts.title: Shortlist recall at beam 16: joint pairs stay at zero in every condition
charts.note: Training lifts marginal single-operator recall, but shuffling inventory descriptions barely hurts it and joint pair recall never leaves zero.

charts.title: Designed observations shrink the consistent-candidate pool, but the model never cashes it in
charts.note: Six designed cases leave far fewer consistent candidate pairs than six random cases, yet this ambiguity reduction did not move model pair recall.

charts.title: Training loss fell but never approached the exact-pair regime
charts.note: Loss drops from 3.29 to the 1.0-1.7 range in the first steps and then plateaus, consistent with format learning without alias-binding learning.

charts.title: One extra query: learned policy vs baselines, by eval split
charts.note: With one query the learned policy beats the hand-coded heuristic on support and IID but loses on the primary ceiling split.

charts.title: Ceiling split: success vs query budget
charts.note: On the primary ceiling split the learned policy trails max-split at budget 1, then catches up to match it at budget 3.

charts.title: Support split: success vs query budget
charts.note: On the hard support split the learned policy leads the heuristic at budgets 1-2 but falls behind at budget 3.

charts.title: Learned choices eliminate far fewer candidates than the oracle action
charts.note: The learned first-query signal is real but weak: its chosen queries eliminate under half as many wrong candidates as the oracle's.
charts.source: report table (Learned Policy Diagnostics), backed by reports/eval/learned_*.json

charts.title: Held-out test accuracy by controller policy
charts.note: Tool-using controllers recover 2/12 held-out cases that direct answering misses; the shuffled-label control collapses to 0.

charts.title: Dev vs held-out test accuracy: the generalization gap
charts.note: Every policy drops sharply from dev to the harder held-out families; even oracle action choice caps at 2/12 on test.

charts.title: Tool budget on held-out test: same accuracy, very different cost
charts.note: All these policies score 2/12, but the LoRA controller gets there with 6 generations and no repairs vs 12+18 for always-tool.

charts.title: Challenge-set repair accuracy: counterexample bridge data fixes the seed adapter
charts.note: Both bridge recipes reach 72/72 reranked vs 46/72 for the seed adapter; static bridge is also perfect under greedy decoding.

charts.title: Where counterexamples help: per-family challenge accuracy
charts.note: The seed adapter's failure is concentrated in length_contains_code (6/24); both bridge conditions repair every family to 24/24.

charts.title: The execution trace is semantically active, not decoration
charts.note: Removing the trace costs 12.5 points; shuffling it collapses accuracy to 36%, showing the model reads the trace, not just its presence.

charts.title: Execution-feedback REPL vs matched-compute sampling (hidden-test accuracy)
charts.note: Execution feedback does not beat independent sampling at matched compute; the feedback content adds only +0.024 over the no-feedback control.

charts.title: Accuracy vs generation budget: sampling curve vs REPL arms
charts.note: The REPL loop at ~4 generations sits on or below the independent-sampling curve — sampling is the stronger use of the same compute.

charts.title: Banking self-solutions: frozen vs self-trained on held-out fresh tasks (N=210)
charts.note: QLoRA-SFT on the model's own 189 verified solutions lifts deployable single-shot +0.095 (~2.2 SE, +42%) with pass@5 rising — no diversity collapse.

charts.title: Expert-iteration flywheel: held-out accuracy by training round
charts.note: Self-training compounds over rounds (+47% greedy@1) with no collapse, but plateaus and never cracks the depth-3 frontier — coverage-bounded.

charts.title: Held-out MBPP coverage vs first-sample quality across training arms
charts.note: Conservative DPO lifts coverage to match K=8 sampling but drops pass@1 by 12.5 points; aggressive DPO collapses entirely.

charts.title: Token cost to reach 66.7% coverage: conservative DPO vs sampling more
charts.note: Both arms hit 66.7% held-out coverage, but conservative DPO at K=4 uses less than half the forward-token budget of K=8 sampling.

charts.title: Parse successes per task: aggressive DPO destroys parseability
charts.note: The 60-step DPO adapters emit zero parseable candidates on held-out tasks, failing the parse guardrail; 10-step training stays safe.

charts.title: Zero-query program selection as the operator library grows
charts.note: The target always stays reachable, but zero-query selection for two-hole programs falls to ~46% at 512 operators as ambiguity grows.

charts.title: Would a small fixed candidate budget still contain the target? (two-hole)
charts.note: Naive fixed budgets collapse for two-hole search past 64 operators (0-12.5% at 512), quantifying what a learned top-k shortlister must beat.

charts.title: Active queries recover selection at 512 operators (two-hole)
charts.note: A few active queries lift selection from 46% to 71-83%, but only after the full N^2 candidate set has already been enumerated.

charts.title: Which two-hole template is hard: low-information comparison gates
charts.note: The comparison-gate template leaves thousands of visible-consistent candidates, so zero-query selection collapses relative to affine-mod.

charts.title: Held-out operator recovery: closed vocabulary vs full inventory
charts.note: Type-colliding held-out operators are unreachable with a closed vocabulary but fully recoverable once the inventory contains them.

charts.title: Active queries close the gap on held-out records (full inventory)
charts.note: One or two disambiguating queries push full-inventory selection from 92.5% to 100%; no budget rescues the closed vocabulary.

charts.title: Per-operator selection accuracy with the full inventory
charts.note: Most operators are near-perfect; gcd and first collide most with other candidates and need active disambiguation.

charts.title: Does the hint add correct-branch preference beyond the student? (delta over no-hint student, nats/token)
charts.note: Gate FAIL: weak hints add ~zero signal on task-specific forks (like shuffled control); their big gain is only on hint-overlap forks.

charts.title: Absolute correct-branch preference on task-specific forks
charts.note: The student already prefers the correct branch by 4.45 nats/token; only the answer-leaking full-reference hint raises it.

charts.title: Rollout-level token pressure on discriminating tokens
charts.note: Broad token view looks optimistic (weak hints upweight correct tokens), but the fork gate shows this is not localized at the correctness bits.

charts.title: Which candidate-selection policy picks hidden-test-passing programs?
charts.note: SFT beats the frozen verifier in-domain on MBPP (+9.4pp) but trails it by 3.2pp on HumanEval transfer; both crush naive selection.

charts.title: How hard is candidate selection? Pool size vs hidden-pass candidates per task
charts.note: MBPP pools are harder: barely half of visible-passing candidates also pass hidden tests, vs ~73% on HumanEval, so selection matters more there.

charts.title: Solving the hidden pair with 3 probes: learned policies vs oracles
charts.note: Mining better probes lifts every arm; SFT gives a real but modest gain over base, while the full-pool oracle shows a large remaining headroom.

charts.title: Success vs number of verifier probes used
charts.note: The full-pool oracle pulls ahead from the very first probe, so the gap is in proposing high-information probes, not in later selection.

charts.title: Easy vs hard template: where the headroom lives
charts.note: pair_compare_gate looks unsolvable to learned policies but reaches 73.8% with full-pool oracle probing — the environment has the needed probes.

charts.title: Solved at 3 probes: trained controllers vs baselines and oracle
charts.note: Posttraining lifts base 32.8% to 43.8% (DPO/GRPO), ~78% of oracle headroom; shuffled-reward and scrambled-feature controls collapse.

charts.title: Solve rate grows with probe budget, trained policies pull ahead
charts.note: All policies start equal at budget 0; trained controllers track the oracle curve more closely at every extra probe.

charts.title: Gains concentrate in the information-rich regime
charts.note: Training helps where probes are informative; the compare-gate regime is identifiability-bounded — even the oracle reaches only 9.4%.

charts.title: Coherence advantage of real thinking grows with budget
charts.note: The overthinking hypothesis is refuted: the value of coherent reasoning order grows from +10.5pp at 512 to +15pp at 2048.

charts.title: Content ladder pass rates across thinking budgets
charts.note: At every budget filler tracks no-think (pure compute is worth ~0), foreign thinking is catastrophic, and coherent real thinking is the entire gain.

charts.title: Held-out MBPP coverage@K: Pass@K RL adapter loses to tuned inference-only sampling
charts.note: The RL adapter (43.8%) undercuts even its own base temperature (50%); tuned-hot sampling at the same K wins outright (68.8%).

charts.title: Guardrail metrics: adapter also degrades first-sample quality and diversity
charts.note: The adapter is worst on both guardrails at K=4; K=8 sampling keeps pass@1 but collapses functional diversity.

charts.title: Why training stalled: most rollout groups were all-fail or all-pass
charts.note: 7 of 16 groups had zero passes and 3 were fully saturated, so only 6 groups gave a usable gradient signal for online Pass@K RL.

charts.title: Hidden-test coverage by search arm (matched completion budget)
charts.note: Gate failed: no prefix arm beats plain full-code sampling on coverage (all top out at 75%), so a prefix value model is not justified.

charts.title: Compute cost of each arm (forward tokens)
charts.note: The oracle-selected prefix matches full-sampling coverage at ~4x fewer tokens, but finding that prefix still required the full sweep.

charts.title: Sample diversity: prefix-conditioned completions vary more
charts.note: Prefix-selected arms produce far more functionally distinct programs per completion, but the extra diversity did not translate into coverage gains.

charts.title: Coverage is the bottleneck, not selection (pass rate at max budget)
charts.note: The SFT verifier captures the full candidate-pool ceiling on both sets; missing MBPP accuracy comes from no correct sample existing, not bad selection.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit_report.md, Key Result + Fixed-Budget Summary)

charts.title: Candidate-pool coverage vs sampling budget
charts.note: HumanEval is nearly solved at 1 sample; MBPP coverage climbs from 35% to 60% and plateaus, leaving a 40% generator-side gap.

charts.title: Adaptive stopping cuts sample cost vs always sampling to max
charts.note: The learned stop controller matches the 96.7% HumanEval ceiling with 1.7 samples instead of 5.5, trading 5pp on MBPP (55% vs 60%) for fewer samples.
charts.source: report table (Adaptive-Budget Summary + Fixed-Budget Summary) / reports/summary_adaptive_budget.csv

charts.title: Which selection policy commits the right answer (max budget)
charts.note: Verifier scoring matches the oracle on both sets; only first-visible and signature-majority drop 5pp on MBPP.

charts.title: Adaptive stopping: what accuracy each controller keeps
charts.note: Learned stopping matches the HumanEval ceiling but gives back 5pp vs oracle stopping on MBPP in exchange for fewer samples.

charts.title: Samples actually drawn vs nominal budget
charts.note: Even fixed-budget runs stop early on visible-test passes: at budget 8, HumanEval draws only 5.4 samples while harder MBPP draws 7.45.

charts.title: Does execution evidence point the teacher at the correct branch?
charts.note: Real execution evidence (+0.063) does not beat shuffled evidence (+0.079); only leaking the answer (+0.237) helps, so the OPSD gate failed.

charts.title: MAP likelihood selector picks worse than first-visible
charts.note: Raw model likelihood selects fewer hidden-correct and more hidden-wrong candidates than simply taking the first visible pass.

charts.title: Lower decoding temperature does not improve retrieval adaptation
charts.note: Greedy decoding slightly raises pass@1 proxy but lowers coverage; no temperature dominates the existing T=0.2 pool.

charts.title: Residual tasks recovered into the candidate pool by retrieval arm
charts.note: Semantic retrieval plus Qwen adaptation recovers a third of direct-sampling misses, doubling the random-retrieval control.

charts.title: Selector commits on residual tasks: correct vs hidden-wrong
charts.note: Every deployable selector commits about as many hidden-wrong candidates as correct ones; only the non-deployable oracle avoids false commits.

charts.title: Constrained subset: oracle arm choice beats sampling more, deployable schedulers don't
charts.note: Oracle per-task arm choice reaches 75% coverage; every deployable schedule ties or trails simply sampling 8 from one policy (66.7%).

charts.title: Broad 80-task pool: learned scheduler loses coverage vs static prefix baseline
charts.note: The learned scheduler (65%) underperforms static schedules (70%); the full union shows 86.2% coverage exists in the pool but costs 8x the tokens.

charts.title: Token cost per constrained arm: oracle headroom comes at half the cost of sampling more
charts.note: Oracle arm choice hits its 75% coverage at 22k tokens, roughly half the 44k spent by base hot @8, which only reaches 66.7%.

charts.title: Mental simulation breaks as pipeline depth grows (frozen model)
charts.note: Exact-match falls from 0.96 at depth 1 to 0.30 at depth 4 — far below the 0.8 kill bar — so the keystone-repair experiment proceeds.

charts.title: Thinking helps at every depth, with and without destructive ops
charts.note: Refutes prediction P-K0b: deliberate simulation is not globally wrong, just length-fragile; destructive ops hurt most without thinking.

charts.title: Decay accelerates: retention per added pipeline step (thinking arm)
charts.note: Retention drops 0.92 to 0.66 to 0.52 per step — no clean single-rate geometric decay, so failure is not one constant per-step error.

charts.title: Does the correct program stay in the candidate set when the task shifts?
charts.note: Coverage survives shift only when the sketch names and types the shifted operator; auto sketches collapse to 17% on primitive shift.

charts.title: Raising the hole-option cap does not rescue auto sketches on primitive shift
charts.note: More candidates per hole only helps manual sketches; auto and erased sketches stay flat because the shifted operator is never proposed.

charts.title: Active queries disambiguate candidates but cannot create missing coverage
charts.y_label: Selected program correct on hidden tests
charts.note: With coverage, 2-3 oracle queries reach 100%; without coverage (primitive shift, auto sketch) extra queries change nothing.

charts.title: Bridge training transfers to held-out ceiling families, but only partially
charts.note: Adding 60 static bridge records saturates support families (120/120) and roughly doubles held-out ceiling transfer (24 to 53 of 120).

charts.title: Ceiling gains depend on a correct execution trace (static 60 controls)
charts.note: Removing the trace drops accuracy to 15% and a shuffled trace is worse than none, so the model is reading the trace, not pattern-matching.

charts.title: Ceiling transfer is bimodal across held-out families
charts.note: Some families jump from 0% to near-perfect, others stay at zero or regress, so bridge transfer is family-specific rather than uniform.

charts.title: Base-miss recovery: strategy-token adapters vs plain sampling at K32
charts.note: The semantic strategy adapter (37.5%) does not beat plain hot K32 (41.7%), and the shuffled-token control matches or beats it.

charts.title: Overall MBPP coverage by sampling arm (80 tasks)
charts.note: Adding semantic strategy samples lands at 81-83% coverage, the same band as one hot K32 arm; only the expensive unions go higher.

charts.title: Hidden-test oracle coverage of 9 residual MBPP tasks by substrate rung
charts.note: Task-specific hand-authored templates cover all 9 residual tasks; reusing retrieved train-split solutions covers none.

charts.title: Public-test trap: share of public-pass candidates that fail hidden tests
charts.note: 40-100% of candidates that pass public tests are secretly wrong, so public tests alone cannot supply trustworthy training targets.

charts.title: Public-pass vs hidden-verified candidates per rung
charts.note: The retrieved-transplant rung produced 8 public-pass candidates and every one failed hidden tests.

charts.title: Candidate cost per residual task by rung
charts.note: The expanded hand-authored rung reaches full coverage at about 15 candidates per task, far cheaper than the combined pool.

charts.title: Accuracy vs mean thinking tokens: escalation controller vs fixed budgets
charts.note: Draft-then-escalate matches fixed-budget accuracy at a fraction of the thinking cost, but never beats the best fixed budget (0.91).

charts.title: Accuracy by strategy: controllers trade ~2pp for big cost cuts
charts.note: Controllers land at 0.88-0.89, between fixed budgets and the 0.93 oracle; only fixed 1024 is more accurate.

charts.title: False-visible-commit rate: the signal that bounds the controller
charts.y_label: committed on visible pass but fails full test
charts.note: 8-11% of controller commits pass the visible test but fail hidden tests - this imperfect signal caps accuracy below the oracle.

charts.title: MBPP accuracy vs thinking-token budget: capped thinking beats both no-think and unlimited
charts.note: Native thinking lifts deployable greedy accuracy +15pp (0.76 to 0.91), more than the oracle ceiling; unbudgeted thinking is worse than a 1024 cap.
charts.source: report table (reports/report.md, main sweep) and README table

charts.title: Rise-then-fall shape holds across metrics as the thinking budget grows
charts.x_label: thinking-token budget cap (0 = no think, 4096 = unbudgeted)
charts.note: Accuracy peaks around a 512-1024 token cap then declines: overthinking has a real cost, so cap the budget rather than deploy unbudgeted.

charts.title: Thinking's gain grows with task difficulty (no-think vs a fixed 1024 budget)
charts.note: The gain scales with difficulty: +6pp on easy, +23pp on middling, and 3 of 9 never-solved tasks become solvable (suggestive, n=9).

charts.title: Shuffled-thinking control: scrambled thinking recovers part of the gain
charts.note: Scrambled thinking recovers much of the greedy lift here, but a later foreign-thinking control showed this understates coherent reasoning's role.

charts.title: MBPP pass rate by thinking condition: only real thinking helps, foreign thinking is catastrophic
charts.note: Filler and shuffled thinking match no-thinking; only coherent real thinking gains +11pp, and another task's thinking collapses to 4%.

charts.title: Attributing the thinking gain: coherent content is the entire effect
charts.y_label: change in pass rate vs previous rung
charts.x_label: ingredient isolated by each ladder step
charts.note: Extra compute and scrambled relevant tokens buy nothing; coherent reasoning content is 100% of the gain, and wrong content is actively harmful.

charts.title: Coherence advantage (real minus shuffled) grows with thinking budget
charts.note: Follow-up sweep refuted the overthinking prediction: the coherent-content advantage grows, not shrinks, at higher budgets.

charts.title: Can a linear probe read correctness off the model's own activations?
charts.note: Thinking makes correctness more decodable, but shuffled thinking matches or beats real thinking - the gain is compute/scaffold, not reasoning.

charts.title: Probe AUC by layer: shuffled thinking tracks or exceeds real thinking everywhere
charts.note: Across all 33 layers, any thinking lifts decodability over no-thinking, and scrambled thinking is at least as good as coherent thinking.

charts.title: Deployable spinoff: ranking true passes among visible-test passers (C2 false-pass regime)
charts.note: Among candidates that already pass the visible test, the probe has weak signal (0.60-0.68) only under thinking; at no-think it is near chance.

charts.title: Held-out accuracy: can a policy pick DIRECT vs PROGRAM from tool state?
charts.note: A simple tool-state rule hits the oracle ceiling (66%); the LoRA policy learns most of the signal (64%) but not all.

charts.title: Recoveries vs losses on held-out test (out of 50 tasks)
charts.note: The learned rule and LoRA policy recover direct misses without ever overwriting a correct direct answer; controls lose 10-21 tasks.

charts.title: LoRA training loss: real labels vs shuffled-label control
charts.note: Real-label loss collapses to near zero while shuffled labels stay unlearnable, confirming the tool-state signal is genuine, not memorization.

charts.title: Zero-coverage tasks recovered per arm (out of 57)
charts.note: At matched token budget, simply sampling more direct solutions recovered more zero-coverage tasks than either repair loop; training made repair worse.

charts.title: Held-out coverage by arm (150 MBPP tasks)
charts.note: Every budget extension beats the direct-x4 base, but sample-more tops both repair arms at essentially the same forward-token cost.

charts.title: False-repair rate: visible-pass candidates that fail hidden tests
charts.note: Training raised the false-repair rate: SFT repair more often produced candidates that pass public tests but fail hidden ones.

charts.title: Filtered execution accuracy: QLoRA scorer closes the oracle gap
charts.note: A small QLoRA on the constrained scorer reaches 48/48, matching the oracle; frozen Qwen misses 4 tasks and non-model baselines are already ~90%.

charts.title: Accuracy by composition depth
charts.note: The QLoRA scorer recovers both depth-2+ composition tasks frozen Qwen missed (19/21 to 21/21) while also perfecting depth-1 selection.

charts.title: Exact target-program match rate
charts.note: Baselines often pass via visible-consistent alternatives; only the QLoRA scorer selects the exact target ABI program every time.

charts.title: Hidden-case success: direct generation vs typed sketch synthesis
charts.note: On the hard ceiling split, typed sketches plus a verifier lift 33% to 88%, and the oracle 100% shows remaining misses are selection, not coverage.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis_report.md); backing files reports/eval/program_*.json, reports/eval/sketch_*.json

charts.title: Ceiling split by task family: direct vs sketch-selected (of 12 each)
charts.note: Sketch synthesis solves whole families direct generation misses entirely, while direct still wins on a few text-gating families.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis_report.md); backing files reports/eval/program_ceiling.json, reports/eval/sketch_ceiling.json

charts.title: Frontier hidden all-pass: bridge data allocation strategies
charts.note: Uniform static bridge coverage (98.3%) beats both active mining variants (~85%) on the broad frontier; seed-only reaches 51.7%.

charts.title: Trace controls: adaptive bridge adapter on the frontier
charts.note: The execution trace carries real signal: removing it costs ~27 points and a shuffled (misleading) trace collapses accuracy to 17.5%.

charts.title: Hardest frontier families: where each allocation strategy breaks
charts.note: Active mining fixes the families it targets but starves others: the adaptive adapter drops to 0% on not_contains_length_code while static coverage holds 100%.

charts.title: Held-out coverage: direct sampling alone vs plus retrieval adaptation
charts.note: Adapting semantically retrieved verified algorithms lifts coverage from 66.7% to 79.2%; random retrieval adds nothing.

charts.title: Direct-sampling misses recovered by each retrieval arm (of 8 misses)
charts.note: Semantic matching recovers 3 misses vs 0 for random and 1 for shuffled, so the retrieved algorithm content matters.

charts.title: Failure mode: visible-pass candidates that fail hidden tests
charts.note: Public tests are too thin: 57-100% of adaptations that pass visible tests still fail hidden tests, motivating a verifier before commit.

charts.title: Held-out ceiling split: hidden all-pass rate by repair policy
charts.note: Verified symbolic edit closure lifts hard held-out repair from ~39% to ~52%, close to the 57.5% oracle ceiling of the same edit neighborhood.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure_report.md, Split Results); matches reports/eval/edit_closure_ceiling.json

charts.title: Where edit closure helps: hidden all-pass records by ceiling family (out of 12)
charts.note: Closure recovers whole families the baseline misses (tuple_value 0 to 9), but two families stay at zero even for the oracle: the edit operators, not the selector, are the limit there.

charts.title: Acceptance policy trade-off: hidden pass-count improved vs damaged records
charts.note: Strict visible-all acceptance halves damage (8 to 4 records) while keeping nearly the same hidden all-pass count (61 vs 62 of 120).

charts.title: Held-out MBPP coverage: self-training arms vs just sampling more
charts.note: One round of verified self-training did not beat the frozen model on held-out tasks; extra inference sampling did (+3.3 pp).

charts.title: Verified self-training gains on train tasks but not on held-out or transfer
charts.note: The +1.3 pp gain on the 80 training tasks does not generalize: held-out and transfer coverage each drop 0.7 pp.

charts.title: Self-training shrinks the candidate pool on held-out tasks
charts.note: Self-trained models emit fewer candidates per task, narrowing the pool that coverage is harvested from.

charts.title: Committed accuracy at budget 5: selection policy matters less than the pool
charts.note: Across every selection policy the self-trained model commits worse answers than the frozen base; the pool, not selection, is binding.

charts.title: A 20-task smoke test flipped sign at full scale
charts.note: The 20-task pilot showed a +5 pp gain for verified self-training that vanished into a slight regression on the full 150-task held-out set.

charts.title: Which selector picks the right candidate? (100 MBPP tasks, k=8 pool)
charts.y_label: full-test pass rate of selected candidate
charts.note: Visible test + a free no-think verifier hits 0.870, tying the 5x-costlier thinking verifier and closing 83% of the pass@1-to-oracle gap.

charts.title: Token cost per task: the thinking verifier's edge is expensive
charts.note: Thinking-verification costs ~5x the visible+no-think combo yet delivers no extra accuracy, so it is Pareto-dominated when a visible test exists.

charts.title: Ranking survivors of the visible test: no-think verification nearly suffices
charts.note: Once the visible test filters candidates, only 6.6% are false-passes and the cheap no-think verifier ranks them almost as well as thinking.

charts.title: Direct bytecode-emission accuracy by training phase
charts.note: Distilling on learned-selected candidates gives modest direct gains (18.0->22.7% on fresh paired), far below the full-supervision ceiling.

charts.title: Answer-search accuracy: distillation gains are largest under search
charts.note: Even imperfect learned targets help most under candidate search: hard-composition search rises from 35.2% to 52.3%.

charts.title: Learned consequence selector roughly matches compiler logprob, far from oracle
charts.note: The selector recovers almost none of the oracle gap (10.2% -> 10.9% vs 36.7% oracle), so selector precision is the bottleneck.

charts.title: Pilot: higher target threshold trades volume for precision
charts.note: Filtering learned targets at score 0.7 halves target count but doubles precision and improves pilot fresh-paired direct accuracy.

charts.title: Per-split direct emission: self-distillation gains are tiny next to the full-supervision ceiling
charts.note: Distilling self-generated candidates lifts direct emission by a few points at most; full supervision reaches 52-82% on the same splits.

charts.title: Program selection vs direct answering on the Qwen-probe subset (120 tasks)
charts.note: Qwen probe labels reach the 25% oracle ceiling but add only 2.5pp over shuffled labels, far below Qwen's 70% direct row answering.

charts.title: Strict diagnostic: row accuracy vs full-task consistency (same 40 tasks)
charts.note: Direct Qwen gets 79.9% of rows right but only 37.5% of whole tasks; crystallized programs manage just 12.5% on the same tasks.

charts.title: Candidate DSL coverage across all static tasks (309 tasks)
categories: oracle: a train-fitting program is held-out valid
charts.note: Every task has train-fitting programs, but only 29.4% have one that generalizes: the bottleneck is held-out validity, not train fit.

charts.title: Oracle DSL ceiling vs examples-only selection by task family
charts.x_label: Task family (10 largest, by task count)
charts.note: The DSL ceiling collapses on the two biggest families, DateTime and Number, so overall coverage is capped well below simpler families.

charts.title: Probe-selection methods across smoke, pilot, and main suites
charts.note: The pattern is stable across suite sizes: probe selection hugs the oracle ceiling with only a thin margin over shuffled labels.

charts.title: DSL oracle ceiling estimate depends on the task sample
charts.note: Oracle coverage swings from 46% to 18% across samples; only the full 309-task run gives a trustworthy 29% ceiling.

charts.title: Held-out task accuracy by example-acquisition policy
charts.note: Actively chosen extra examples lift task accuracy from 66.7% to 70.0%; random or label-shuffled extras hurt, and the oracle ceiling is 73.3%.

charts.title: Accuracy vs number of acquired examples, by selection strategy
charts.note: Only active selection stays above the 4-example baseline at both budgets; random and diverse acquisition can make things worse.

charts.title: DateTime tasks: the largest hard family under example acquisition
charts.note: On DateTime, the largest hard family (13 tasks), one actively chosen example lifts accuracy 46.2% to 53.8%, while random extras drop it to 38.5%.

charts.title: Row-level vs full-task strict accuracy by acquisition policy
charts.note: Full-task exact runs 11-18 points below row exact for every policy; active selection narrows the gap most.

charts.title: Where the headroom is: base vs oracle full-task accuracy by family
charts.note: Six of nine families are already saturated at 100%; extra examples can only move DateTime and Number, and Meteorite stays at 0%.

charts.title: Number family: three extra examples help, but only if labels are real
charts.note: On Number tasks, active3 and diverse3 hit the oracle ceiling (83.3%) while shuffled-label examples drag accuracy below base.

charts.title: Probe-variant sensitivity: leave-one-out accuracy on tasks where probes disagree
charts.note: Probe variants mostly agree, but on hard tasks the consistency probe can swing leave-one-out accuracy by 25 points either way.

charts.title: Batched transduction vs row-by-row prompting (40 text-transformation tasks)
charts.note: No batched arm beats row-by-row on strict full-task exact; larger batches help but the best (45%) still trails row-by-row (50%).

charts.title: Stricter batch prompts do not recover the gap
charts.note: A verify hint ties plain batch-all at 45% full-task exact and structured JSON input falls to 42.5%; prompt iteration cannot close the gap.

charts.title: Per-task outcome: batch-all vs row-by-row on full-task exact
charts.note: Batch-all never flips a failed task into a full-task success and loses two tasks that row-by-row solved: a clean negative result.

charts.title: Top-1 accuracy: learned value guidance vs answer-verified repair
charts.note: Learned budgeted action values give only small top-1 gains; the answer-verified repair oracle stays 12-22 points ahead.

charts.title: Candidate sets already contain the answer: beam top-1 vs candidate oracle
charts.note: The beam generates correct programs far more often than it ranks them first; selection, not generation, is the bottleneck.

charts.title: Value-weight sweep on fresh paired tasks: the useful range is narrow
charts.note: Small value weights nudge accuracy above the 67.2% logprob baseline, but over-weighting the learned value hurts beam ranking.

charts.title: How learnable is each action-value target? Held-out AUC in the main run
charts.note: Sparse exact-prefix labels are easy to discriminate; the richer graded targets are much harder for the lightweight value head.

charts.title: Compiler quick bytecode accuracy during training: main run vs pilot
charts.note: The pilot compiler stalled near 12.5%; quadrupling supervised traces let the main run reach 71.9% quick bytecode accuracy.

charts.title: Exact program match by split: value beams barely move it, repair helps modestly
charts.note: Exact recovery of the canonical program stays far below answer accuracy on every split, collapsing to ~30% on hard composition.

charts.title: Prefix-action label rates by run: recoverable actions dwarf canonical ones
charts.note: In the main run only 4.3% of actions keep the canonical trace, yet 72.1% can still be completed to the right answer.

charts.title: Held-out AUC across value-model training epochs: plateaus almost immediately
charts.note: Each target's AUC is set by epoch 1 and drifts flat or down over 8 epochs; the head, not training time, limits graded-value quality.

charts.title: Standard split: no trained selector beats the no-repair base policy
charts.note: Oracles show 31-46pp of reachable repair headroom, but every deployable selector ties base at best; ungated Qwen selectors collapse.

charts.title: Validation gating stops the damage but recovers no gains (standard split)

series.label: damage rate (broke a correct base program)

charts.note: Ungated Qwen selectors over-edit and break ~86-97% of correct base programs; the base-fallback gate removes the damage only by barely editing.

charts.title: Repair headroom exists on every evaluation split
charts.note: The candidate pool contains correct repairs on every split (full-pool oracle 14-50pp above base), so the failure is selection, not coverage.

charts.title: Ungated Qwen selectors rewrite almost everything on the standard split
charts.note: Ungated Qwen arms edit ~92-98% of groups and damage most correct programs; oracles edit only where a repair exists.

charts.title: Trace corruption and label shuffling barely change the failure
charts.note: Real candidate-reading Qwen heads end training near the shuffled-label and corrupted-trace controls, on both seeds.

charts.title: Base-policy accuracy swings wildly across source seeds
charts.note: Seed 789 is near ceiling while seed 123 hits 0% on standard, so repair headroom concentrates in specific source seeds.

charts.title: On the held-out source, gating is the only thing standing between Qwen and collapse
charts.note: With base already at 91.7%, ungated Qwen selectors fall to 0-12.5%; gated variants recover 87.5-91.7% but never beat base.

charts.title: Exact execution on fresh length-24 splits: verifier recovers 35-46% of the oracle gap
charts.note: A learned trace verifier picks repairs without seeing true answers, lifting accuracy from ~29% to 50-56% (35-46% of the oracle gap).

charts.title: Fresh paired split: pair reranking mainly buys cross-paraphrase consistency
charts.note: Independent verification hurts pair consistency (71% to 58%); reranking the pair jointly restores it to 87% and lifts both-correct to 52%.

charts.title: Verifier accuracy on fresh splits grows with training-set size
charts.note: Across the s128/s192 pilots and the s512 main run (configs differ slightly), fresh-split accuracy rises steadily with more training programs.

charts.title: Fresh length-24 retest: state-loss schedules vs no state loss
charts.y_label: accuracy on fresh paired length-24 programs
charts.note: Keeping light state supervision in the long stage beats no state loss; turning it fully off in the long stage is worst.

charts.title: Checkpoint selection is necessary: selected step 800 vs final step 900
charts.note: Every arm collapses by the final training step; reporting the best validation checkpoint roughly doubles length-24 execution.

charts.title: Fresh length-24 retest by rendering: paraphrase robustness
charts.y_label: execution accuracy on fresh length-24 programs
charts.note: Standard-rendering accuracy is similar across arms; state supervision in the long stage mainly protects paraphrased programs.

charts.title: Length-24 program execution accuracy by training arm (mean of 3 seeds)
charts.note: Copied structural expansion has the best mean on most splits; same-budget no-curriculum training nearly fails on standard wording.

charts.title: Standard length-24 accuracy per seed: means hide huge seed variance
charts.note: The best arm ranges from total failure to 81% across seeds, so no single-seed run supports a stable attribution claim.

charts.title: Partial execution is not the bottleneck: state-prefix recovery vs exact execution (standard L24, mean of 3 seeds)
charts.note: Every arm recovers long state prefixes far more often than exact programs; the failure is late-step global consistency, not local operators.

charts.title: Capability cliff between length 16 and length 24
charts.note: Nearly every run solves length-16 programs, then falls off a cliff at length 24 -- the failure is length-specific, not general.

charts.title: Training looks identical across seeds even when eval collapses
charts.note: All three curriculum seeds train to near-perfect state accuracy, yet seed 456 scores 1.6% at eval -- the collapse is invisible in training.

charts.title: Standard wording is the hardest split: paraphrase templates score far higher
charts.note: Most runs execute paraphrased length-24 programs far better than standard wording -- some hit 92-100% paraphrase with near-zero standard.

charts.title: Answering both paraphrases of the same program is much rarer than answering one
charts.note: Pair-level consistency collapses relative to per-item accuracy in every run except the strongest seed (expand_copy 789).

charts.title: Program selection accuracy: learned verifier vs frozen compiler vs oracle
charts.note: Correct candidates exist for ~91-100% of prompts, but the learned verifier gains little in-distribution and loses on longer chains.

charts.title: Accuracy vs program length on standard splits
charts.note: The oracle stays near-perfect as programs get longer, but the learned verifier falls below the frozen compiler beyond length 6.

charts.title: Share of the base-to-oracle gap recovered by the learned verifier
charts.note: The verifier recovers at most 5.6% of the reachable headroom and goes negative on paired and longer-chain splits.

charts.title: Per-domain accuracy on length-6 prompts
charts.note: Modest verifier gains in four domains, no change on lookup, and a 9.4-point loss on boolean, where answers collapse to few values.

charts.title: Differentiable soft-executor scorer trails the frozen compiler on every split
charts.note: The differentiable soft-executor scoring heuristic loses to just keeping the compiler argmax on all splits except the hardest paraphrase one.

charts.title: Paraphrased prompts widen the accuracy drop as programs get longer
charts.note: Paraphrasing costs ~11-23 points at every length, and the learned verifier makes the paraphrase drop worse, not better, at lengths 8-10.

charts.title: Held-out deep composition: execution accuracy by training curriculum (constrained decoding)
charts.note: Adding depth-2/3 composition examples lifts held-out depth-6 and depth-8 execution by 11.1 points over atomic-only training.

charts.title: Execution accuracy across procedure depth (constrained decoding)
charts.note: Only the d1+d2+d3 curriculum recovers as depth grows, reaching 88.9% at depth 8; the other arms fall to 72.2% by depth 6.

charts.title: Failure taxonomy at depths 6-8: where valid programs go wrong
charts.note: The d1+d2+d3 curriculum nearly halves wrong-operation-order errors (25.0% to 13.9%), showing a semantic gain, not just syntax.

charts.title: Decoder comparison at depth 8: constrained decoding beats free and resample

charts.title: Depth-6 execution accuracy: constrained decoding beats free decoding and cheap resampling
charts.note: At held-out depth 6, finite-state constrained decoding reaches 75% execution vs 60% free — resampling to validity only gets 62.5%.

charts.title: Execution accuracy vs procedure depth (held-out compositions)
charts.note: The constrained decoder leads free decoding by 10-20 points at every depth; resampling tracks free decoding closely.

charts.title: Wording-shifted prompts: constrained decoding is robust, free decoding degrades
charts.note: Under template shift the constrained decoder holds 75-83% while free decoding falls to ~48% and the parse stage collapses.

charts.title: Depth 6: validity vs execution vs correct-given-valid
charts.note: The grammar forces 100% validity and lifts execution to 75%, but some forced-valid programs are wrong (correct-given-valid drops to 75%).

charts.title: Program selection accuracy: learned verifier barely moves the base compiler while oracle headroom stays near 100%
charts.note: Answer-correct candidates exist for ~98% of prompts, but the learned verifier gains only 1-2 points in-distribution and loses on harder splits.

charts.title: Fraction of the base-to-oracle gap the verifier recovers, by split
charts.note: The verifier recovers at most 5.6% of available headroom in-distribution and goes negative on paired and longer-chain splits.

charts.title: Per-domain accuracy on length-6 prompts: modest gains except boolean, which the verifier hurts
charts.note: Boolean drops 9.4 points: many candidates share the few possible boolean answers, so answer-only labels give little trace-identifying signal.
