charts.note: Answer-only loss on counterfactual episodes converges within ~40 steps of the 120-step run.

charts.title: Training scale: full-task exact match, pilot (240 episodes) vs main (800 episodes)
charts.note: Scaling 240 to 800 train episodes lifts public transfer and makes shuffled support hurt more, evidence of real support conditioning.

charts.title: Public text-transformation accuracy: base model vs LoRA-tuned adapters
charts.note: Synthetic ICL-style LoRA tuning triples public accuracy, but the ordinary and shuffled-label controls match it — the counterfactual ingredient isn't isolated.

charts.title: Adapter accuracy depends on the in-context support examples
charts.note: Corrupting or removing support examples collapses performance, so adapters do read the examples — but shuffled-label training still yields high shuffled-support accuracy.

charts.title: Synthetic training gains transfer to the public benchmark
charts.note: Training only on synthetic episodes with answer-only supervision lifts unseen public tasks by 40.7 points, not just the synthetic generator.

charts.title: Per-seed public task flips versus base (45 tasks)
charts.note: The gain is consistent across seeds: about 20 of 45 public tasks improve while only 1 regresses in every seed.

charts.title: Which public task families the adapter fixes
charts.note: Gains span structured families (codes, paths, currency); DateTime stays hardest and the single Address task regresses to 0%.

charts.title: Shuffled-label training control still tolerates shuffled support
charts.note: The adapter trained on shuffled labels keeps 91.7% under shuffled support, so a formatting explanation for the gains remains plausible.

charts.title: Strict whole-task scoring costs every arm on the public benchmark
charts.note: Requiring every row of a task to match costs adapters about 10 points but nearly halves the base model's score.

charts.title: Direct accuracy after distillation: preference-selected vs answer-verified targets
charts.note: Preference-selected distillation gives small direct gains on the hardest splits, but every repair branch sits far below the supervised ceiling.

charts.title: Preference selector picks worse than the base decode on held-out splits
charts.note: The learned selector stays valid but misses the correct answer: reranking never beats the base decode, while the candidate oracle shows large headroom.

charts.title: Why preference distillation is noisy: answer-correct precision of training targets
charts.note: Preference-selected targets cover all 1024 prompts but only 12.2% are answer-correct, versus 100% for the 466 verified targets.

charts.title: The trade-off: preference-selected targets degrade search on fresh paired
charts.note: Preference-selected distillation buys small direct gains at the cost of search accuracy, which drops below even the undistilled seed model.

charts.title: Full-supervised ceiling vs the seed compiler across all held-out splits
charts.note: With answer labels the compiler reaches 91% direct on fresh paired but drops to 68% on hard composition; the seed sits near 14%.

charts.title: Program exact match: repair distillation barely changes program identity
charts.note: All repair-distilled branches stay under 4% exact-program recovery while full supervision reaches 52-78%.

charts.title: Selector iterations: candidate features helped the pilot, scaling lost the held-out gain
charts.note: Feature-bridged pilot selection roughly doubled the bytecode-only pilot, but the scaled main run kept only the validation signal.

charts.title: Accuracy by output format across evaluation splits
charts.note: Python and stack traces edge out answer-only on fresh values, but every format collapses on unseen operation compositions.

charts.title: Token cost of each output format (fresh-value eval)
charts.note: Traces cost 3-6.5x more tokens than answer-only for at most a ~2pp accuracy gain on fresh values.

charts.title: Format validity: JSON is harder to emit reliably
charts.note: Line-oriented formats always parse; the verbose JSON object fails to yield a valid FINAL value roughly a quarter of the time.

charts.title: Where composition fails: python trace accuracy by task family (unseen compositions)
charts.note: Composition failures concentrate in string and date tasks that need new operation combinations; direct unit conversion stays easy.

charts.title: Template-shift accuracy by task family: JSON fails whole families
charts.note: JSON's template-shift weakness is not uniform: it scores 0% on table and date tasks while other formats stay above 83% on most families.

charts.title: Final VM controller vs baselines and oracle across evaluation splits
charts.note: The trained VM controller beats native Qwen on 4 of 5 splits (all but Standard) yet stays far below the oracle teacher.

charts.title: Accuracy vs rollout budget K (final checkpoint, learned stopping)
charts.note: Accuracy dips at short budgets (partial edits hurt) then jumps at K=8; extra steps beyond K=8 help little and are not monotonic.
charts.source: report table (reports/report.md), Final Learned Accuracy by K; backed by runs/main_joint_action_calibrated_s256_r2/results.json

charts.title: State-level supervision through BC and DAgger rounds
charts.note: DAgger keeps improving supervised targets; argument prediction stays the weakest component even after two rounds.

charts.title: Rollout accuracy after each training phase (K=8, learned stopping)
charts.note: DAgger rounds lift hard composition steadily (18.8% to 31.2%) but shuffle rather than improve the other splits.

charts.title: False STOP rate grows with rollout budget (final checkpoint)
charts.note: By K=12 the learned controller stops early on 41-56% of tasks, capping the gains from longer rollouts.

charts.title: Learned vs value-gated stopping at K=12
charts.note: Value gating adds about 3 points on Mixed and Standard but costs 3 points on Hard, so neither stopping rule dominates.

charts.title: Pilot runs (96 tasks) vs main run (256 tasks), final checkpoint at K=8
charts.note: Joint action heads and 2.7x more training tasks were both needed: the pilot text-action agent barely gets off the floor.

charts.title: Full-task exact match: gated program use ties across probe variants
charts.note: The simple direct-fallback gate lifts 56% to 64%; disagreement probes add nothing beyond it (probe and random-probe gates also 64%).

charts.title: Tasks helped vs hurt relative to direct answering (25 tasks)
charts.note: Raw generated programs hurt 6-7 tasks; the conservative fallback gates keep the 2 wins while hurting none.

charts.title: Full-task exact by task family: direct vs probe-gated
charts.note: All of the gated method's gain comes from Number tasks; every other family matches direct answering exactly.

charts.title: Row-level exact match by method
charts.note: At the row level all gated variants tie at 82.7%; ungated program arms roughly halve accuracy and shuffled labels collapse to 6%.

charts.title: Share of tasks with a train-passing program used or found, by method
charts.note: Programs pass visible examples on 52% of tasks, but the conservative gates only trust them on 40-44%; shuffled-label probes trust none.

charts.title: Row exact by task family: direct answering vs raw generated programs
charts.note: Ungated programs match direct answering on Currency/Email/Phone/Rating but collapse on BillingCode, FilePath, and especially DateTime.

charts.title: Held-out accuracy by method: learned soft prompt vs direct inference and controls
charts.note: The learned per-task soft prompt ties direct row-by-row inference on full-task exact (52.5%) and beats the shuffled-label control, but never beats direct.

charts.title: Change vs direct row-by-row baseline (full-task exact)
charts.note: No condition beats the direct baseline on fully solved tasks; the learned prefix is neutral (1 task helped, 1 hurt, 38 tied).

charts.title: Soft-prompt training loss: real labels vs shuffled labels
charts.note: The prefix optimizes cleanly on real labels (mean loss 1.06 to 0.25 in 6 steps), so the null result is not a failure to train.
charts.source: report table 'Train Loss Log' (per-step means of analysis/train_log.csv)

charts.title: Tasks helped vs hurt relative to direct row-by-row (40-task run)
charts.note: Almost every task ties the direct baseline; the learned prefix helps 1 task and hurts 1, while controls only hurt.

charts.title: Row-level accuracy change vs direct row-by-row baseline (40-task run)

series.label: Change in row exact vs direct row

charts.note: At row level the untrained init prefix gains as much as the learned one, so training adds nothing beyond having any prefix.

charts.title: Held-out row accuracy at 20-task vs 40-task evaluation budgets
charts.note: Method ordering is roughly stable across budgets: row-by-row decoding beats batch, and the learned prefix edge over direct stays tiny.

charts.title: Deep-composition accuracy by training curriculum (constrained decoding)
charts.note: Training on compositions only up to depth 3 already yields 94-100% at depths 12-16; deeper curricula add nothing (depth 8 even hurts).

charts.title: Execution accuracy across evaluation depth, per curriculum
charts.note: Only atomic-only training decays with depth; any compositional supervision (even max depth 3) extrapolates flat out to depth 16.

charts.title: Constrained vs free decoding at depth 16
charts.note: Finite-state constrained decoding adds 17-39 points over free generation at depth 16; the semantics are there, syntax was the bottleneck.

charts.title: Failure taxonomy at depths 12-16 (constrained decoding)
charts.note: Compositional supervision cuts wrong-operation-order errors from 31% to at most 8%; atomic-only training composes valid but semantically wrong programs 31% of the time.

charts.title: More latent recurrent steps do not help: 250-example retest of the best checkpoint
charts.x_label: invisible recurrent steps K at evaluation
charts.note: On the strongest checkpoint (step 200), K=0 beats every K>0 budget on both splits at n=250 — the hoped-for K-scaling is absent.

charts.title: Promising gains at 100 examples evaporate at 250 examples (step-200 checkpoint)
charts.note: The +4-5pp recurrence advantage seen in small checkpoint sweeps reverses on larger retests — the apparent gains were sampling noise.

charts.title: Best recurrence gain over training is unstable, and an untrained-recurrence control bumps too
charts.note: K gains flicker across checkpoints and a control that never trained the recurrent core shows similar bumps, so isolated gains are noise.

charts.title: Full-task exact accuracy: table selection methods vs the oracle ceiling
charts.note: The exact table is reachable on 62.5% of tasks, but no selector - learned or not - beats greedy row decoding at 50%.

charts.title: Row-level vs strict full-task accuracy by method
charts.note: Rows are ~72% correct but strict all-rows-correct scoring drops every non-oracle method to 50% or below.

charts.title: Reranker discrimination per cross-validation fold: real vs shuffled labels
charts.note: The reranker genuinely ranks correct tables highly (AUC ~93% vs chance), yet that never converts into solving extra tasks.

charts.title: Full-task accuracy by data family: where the oracle headroom lives
charts.note: Nearly all reachable headroom sits in Number tasks (30% vs 70%); Address and Phone families leave the oracle nothing to add.

charts.title: Row-level accuracy by data family: sampled candidates fix Number rows
charts.note: Candidate sampling recovers many Number rows (61.7% to 89.2%) and some DateTime rows, but Address and City rows never improve.

charts.title: The five reachable-headroom tasks the learned reranker failed to capture
charts.note: On every task where a perfect candidate table exists, the learned reranker picks the same table as greedy and captures 0 of 5 wins.

charts.title: Cross-validation label shift: positive-candidate rate per fold
charts.note: Test positive rates swing from 6.5% to 48.2% across folds, so high candidate AUC coexists with unstable task-level selection.

charts.title: Decision updates reduced deployed accuracy vs behavior cloning
charts.note: Both decision-optimization arms lowered learned-mode accuracy below the behavior-cloned baseline; the oracle teacher sits at 82.5%.

charts.title: K=8 split accuracy collapsed after the update
charts.note: The GRPO+ECHO update drove K=8 accuracy to 0% across evaluation splits; process preference also fell sharply.

charts.title: Process-preference update doubled false STOPs
charts.note: GRPO reduced false STOPs but shifted errors to invalid slot edits; DPO doubled false STOPs — both signs of broken action/STOP calibration.

charts.title: Training diagnostics: STOP accuracy degraded even as other signals looked healthy
charts.note: Both decision updates dragged STOP accuracy from 85% to under 30%, despite DPO reaching 80.2% pairwise ranking accuracy in training.

charts.title: Headroom per split: learned policy sits near the blank baseline while oracle search solves most tasks
charts.note: On every split the behavior-cloned policy barely beats leaving the program blank, while oracle search reaches 75-94%.

charts.title: Oracle search scales with rollout budget K on all five splits
charts.note: The VM plus oracle-guided search climbs from near-baseline to 75-94% as K grows, so the capability ceiling is in the policy, not the task.

charts.title: GRPO update broke edit validity per split; process-DPO kept edits valid but still lost accuracy
charts.note: The GRPO update roughly halved valid-edit rates on every split, while process-DPO produced 100% valid edits yet still hurt accuracy.

charts.title: Per-head training accuracy across phases: action sub-heads stayed near BC levels while deployment collapsed
charts.note: Both decision updates left per-head training accuracy near BC epoch-1 levels, so the deployment collapse was not a per-head fitting failure.

charts.title: Fresh paired accuracy: repair distillation hurts the deployable compiler
charts.note: The hidden VM beats direct logits, but repair distillation loses 36.7 pp vs the trace-only control; nearby verified programs exist 99.6% of the time.

charts.title: Length robustness: trace-only control degrades gracefully, repair-distilled collapses
charts.note: Beyond the trained length 6, the trace-only curriculum still holds 55% and 30% at lengths 8 and 10, while the repair-distilled model falls to near-baseline.

charts.title: Fresh paired accuracy by domain (repair-distilled run)
charts.note: The hidden VM beats direct logits in every domain except boolean, and verified repair finds a correct nearby program almost everywhere.

charts.title: Training phases: the repair-distillation step itself causes the collapse
charts.note: Both runs are identical at step 700 (71.9%); the 220-step repair-distillation phase alone drops fresh paired accuracy to 35.2%.

charts.title: Verified repair ceiling stays high even where the compiler collapses
charts.note: Even at length 10 where the argmax compiler scores under 10%, a correct executable program sits in the local neighborhood 88-96% of the time.

charts.title: Program fidelity across runs: repair distillation erodes exact programs
charts.note: Trace-only training keeps 55.9% exact programs; repair distillation cuts that to 18.0%, and the length-6-only pilot ablation is similarly weak.

charts.title: Hidden VM compiler vs direct answering on fresh mixed-domain tasks
charts.note: Compiling prompts into a hidden executable VM beats direct answering by 62.9 pp; trace supervision adds 17.6 pp over answer-only.

charts.title: Per-domain accuracy: direct answering vs hidden VM (fresh paired)
charts.note: The hidden VM lifts every domain; four domains go from near 0% direct accuracy to 67-88%, with calendar the weakest transfer.

charts.title: Trace supervision yields cleaner programs than answer-only training
charts.note: Answer-only gradients still learn a latent executor, but trace supervision nearly doubles exact-program recovery (63.7% vs 34.4%).

charts.title: Length generalization: trained on lengths 1-4, tested at length 6
charts.note: The compiler transfers beyond training length but degrades at length 6, especially under paraphrase - not yet a length-general algorithm.

charts.title: More trace-training examples sharply improve the hidden VM compiler
charts.note: Going from 160 to 512 training examples lifts hidden VM accuracy from 15.6% to 77.7%; the compiler is still data-hungry at 4B.

charts.title: Training lifts the hidden VM, not direct answering (fresh paired)
charts.note: Posttraining moves hidden VM execution from 28.9% to 77.7% and program-exact from 0% to 63.7%, while direct answering barely budges.
charts.source: experiments/qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains/runs/main_hidden_vm_trace_s512/train_log.csv (step 0 baseline row) and analysis/all_final_metrics.csv (fresh_paired_mixed final row)

charts.title: Fresh paired mixed accuracy: direct answer vs hidden VM vs repair headroom
charts.note: Compiling to a hidden VM lifts accuracy from 8.6% to ~61%, but on-policy repair training adds only +2.0pp over the trace-only control.

charts.title: Length generalization: on-policy repair does not improve hard-length robustness
charts.note: Beyond the trained length 6, the on-policy repair arm falls at or below the trace-only control, while verified local search keeps large headroom.

charts.title: Main training arms are indistinguishable on fresh paired accuracy
charts.note: All repair-target arms land on the same result as gold-only training, so distilling verified repairs adds nothing over the controls.

charts.title: Per-domain accuracy on fresh paired tasks
charts.note: The hidden VM beats direct answering in every domain, and verified local repair finds a correct program for most remaining failures.

charts.title: Paraphrase wording hurts more as tasks get longer
charts.note: Paraphrasing costs ~11 points at fresh lengths but ~26-32 points at hard/harder lengths, and repair cannot close the gap.

charts.title: Pilot ablations: repair-heavy training trades compiler quality for repair rate
charts.note: Training on repair targets lifts repaired accuracy to 96.9% but drops one-shot compiler accuracy from 63.5% to 50.0%.

charts.title: The repair search itself collapses on harder paraphrased tasks
charts.note: Verified repair finds a fix for 94.8% of fresh tasks but only 26.0% of harder paraphrased ones, so headroom vanishes exactly where needed.

charts.title: What training buys over the base model on held-out validation
charts.note: The base model never emits an exactly-correct program; training lifts repaired accuracy from 28% to 95% on validation.

charts.title: Hard-composition accuracy across training phases
charts.note: VM-ECHO lifts hard-composition answer search from 47.7% to 51.6%, but direct accuracy stays far below the 58.6% full-supervised ceiling.

charts.title: Fresh-paired direct accuracy: VM-ECHO does not move the needle
charts.note: Fresh-paired direct accuracy is stuck at 8.6% through VM-ECHO training while the full-supervised ceiling is 76.6%.

charts.title: What the VM-ECHO head learned to predict (fresh standard)
charts.note: ECHO learned structural consequences (trace depth 91.5%, validity 72.9%) far better than which candidate answers correctly (49.9%).

charts.title: ECHO reranking underperforms direct decoding on every split
charts.note: The learned consequence model is not a useful reranker: selecting candidates by ECHO score loses to plain direct decoding everywhere.

charts.title: Pilot ablations: the VM-ECHO gain vanishes at higher weight or a second round
charts.note: The pilot gain that motivated the main run appears only at weight 0.10 with one round; 0.35 and a second round erase it.

charts.title: Answer-search accuracy by phase: VM-ECHO nudges the search surface, full supervision transforms it
charts.note: VM-ECHO lifts search on 3 of 4 splits (hard comp 47.7%→51.6%) but full supervision reaches 86-95%, far above any distill phase.

charts.title: Learned iterative repair converts near-miss compiled programs into exact ones (fresh paired prompts)
charts.note: Iterative one-edit-at-a-time repair lifts fresh paired accuracy from 30.1% to 52.7%, recovering 38% of the gap to the local oracle.

charts.title: Repair iterations K: most of the gain arrives by K=1, saturates at K=2
charts.note: One repair step gives the bulk of the improvement; K=3 adds nothing because candidates are capped at two edits from the base program.

charts.title: Nested supervision transfers to wider held-out branch counts
charts.note: Shallow nested supervision (2-3 branches) generalizes: nested-trained models hit 100% at 4 branches and 73-87% at 8; chain-only models collapse.

charts.title: Growing the operation library from 32 to 128 does not hurt linear chains
charts.note: Constrained chain compilation stays at or near 100% out to depth 16 even with a 4x larger primitive library.

charts.title: Constrained decoding beats free decoding on hardest nested tasks (8 branches)
charts.note: Finite-state constraints roughly double nested-8 accuracy for nested-trained models, but cannot rescue chain-only training.

charts.title: Template-shifted wording degrades nested tasks more than chains
charts.note: Under reworded prompts the best nested model keeps 73% on chains but drops to 40% on nested-8: wording robustness is unsolved for nesting.

charts.title: Exact program recovery vs program length (single compiler, max-24 mixed training)
charts.note: The compiler writes exact executable programs up to 12 steps, but 24-step behavior is brittle: 68.8% on paraphrase, 0% on standard.

charts.title: Beam search failed where a single supervised compiler succeeded (length-12 standard split)
charts.note: Beam runs stay at chance even for the oracle, so beams never produced correct candidate programs; one supervised compiler solves the split.
charts.source: reports/aggregate_final_metrics.csv (standard_len12 rows for pilot_beam4_s120, pilot_latent_beam4_s160, pilot_single_compiler_len24_unpaired_s600, main_single_compiler_len24_paired_s750)

charts.title: Length-24 failure mode after paired-template training: long correct prefixes, late errors
charts.note: The paired-trained compiler executes ~85% of the 24-step trace correctly but almost never finishes exactly, so paired consistency alone did not fix depth.

charts.title: Length-24 accuracy during training: gains arrive late and are unstable
charts.note: The mixed run's 24-step win appears only at the final step; the paired run peaks at 23% mid-training and collapses back to zero.

charts.title: Why beams failed: even the oracle over 4 latent beams is near chance
charts.note: Oracle accuracy never exceeds 4.7%, so the beams contained no correct programs — the failure was candidate generation, not selection.

charts.title: Paired-template training erased the mixed run's 24-step win
charts.note: Paired training raised standard-24 trace prefixes from 49% to 85% yet dropped paraphrase-24 answers from 69% to 3% — no net win.
charts.source: reports/aggregate_final_metrics.csv (len24 rows for pilot_single_compiler_len24_unpaired_s600 and main_single_compiler_len24_paired_s750)

charts.title: Max-8 compiler baseline: paraphrase prompts were easier than standard ones
charts.note: The first working compiler already showed template sensitivity: standard prompts lose 13 points at length 8 while paraphrase holds at 94.5%.

charts.title: Full-task accuracy at a 4-example label budget: Qwen's chosen examples tie fixed-order
charts.note: Extra labels help (63.3% to 70%) and shuffled labels hurt, but Qwen's selections only match a trivial fixed-order policy; oracle shows 80% headroom.

charts.note: Extra revealed labels steadily lift DateTime tasks but drag Number tasks down, so family mix hides opposite trends at budget 4.

charts.title: Row-level accuracy hides the strict full-task gap

charts.title: Learned repair verifier lifts executor accuracy on fresh length-24 tasks

charts.title: Paired split: pair-consistency reranking adds further gains over the learned verifier
charts.note: Enforcing answer consistency across paraphrase pairs raises paired accuracy to 51% and both-correct to 46.5%, well above the learned verifier alone.

charts.title: Exact-answer accuracy on length-4 programs: trace supervision vs controls
charts.note: Explicit trace supervision makes hidden states compile to correct answers (33-41%); answer-only training stays near zero.

charts.title: Executor accuracy collapses with program length
charts.note: Per-step argument errors compound: exact execution is near zero by 12 steps even though the interface works at length 4.

charts.title: Which program symbols the parser decodes (main condition, standard prompts)
charts.note: Initial values and operations decode almost perfectly; noisy argument-value decoding (~80% and falling) is the bottleneck.

charts.title: Executable bytecode accuracy on fresh paired prompts, by training condition

charts.title: Direct generation vs answer-verified local search (main QLoRA trace run)
charts.note: Verifying candidate programs against the answer adds 13-20 pp on every split, lifting fresh paired from 68% to 86%.

charts.title: Expert-iteration target quality across rounds
charts.note: The expert finds verified targets for more prompts each round while rewriting fewer of the model's own outputs, a sign of convergence.

charts.title: Executable bytecode accuracy by evaluation split
charts.note: All conditions drop most on hard compositions; dense trace supervision leads on every split, expert iteration trails everywhere.

charts.title: Verifier reranking lifts frozen compiler accuracy on fresh paired length-6 prompts

charts.title: Length-6-to-8 verifier gains generalize across held-out splits

charts.title: Pair reranking makes paired standard+paraphrase prompts jointly correct more often

charts.title: Share of the oracle selection gap the learned verifier recovers

charts.title: Where the QLoRA compiler loses accuracy (standard prompts)
charts.note: Initial values and operations are solved almost perfectly; small per-slot argument errors compound into the whole-program exact-match falloff.

charts.title: Compiler accuracy before vs after on-policy repair training
charts.note: One round of trace-level fine-tuning lifts the deployable compiler from ~29% to ~99% on fresh prompts, nearly closing the repair-ceiling gap.

charts.title: Attribution controls: what training signal drives the gain (fresh paired split)
charts.note: Gold trace supervision alone matches the main run at 99.2%; verified repairs alone still reach 91.0%, so repairs are a real but weaker deployable signal.

charts.title: Local repair headroom at baseline vs after training
charts.note: The target-aware repair ceiling itself rises from ~65-70% to 100% after training, since local edits repair a near-correct compiler far more easily.

charts.title: Final compiler accuracy by evaluation split and training recipe
charts.note: Repair-only training lags most on paraphrased prompts (83% vs 97%); gold-only matches the mixed recipe on every split.

charts.title: Paraphrase-pair consistency before vs after on-policy training
charts.note: Training nearly closes the paraphrase-robustness gap: pairs answered correctly on both phrasings jump from 27% to 98%.
charts.source: runs/main_onpolicy_repair_s256/train_log.csv (fresh_paired_len24 pair metrics, baseline and train rows)

charts.title: Residual fixable errors after training: how often repair still edits the program
charts.y_label: Fraction of programs changed by repair
charts.note: Remaining locally-repairable errors concentrate on paraphrased prompts, especially for the repair-only control (16%) and the small pilot.

charts.title: Task solve rate by acquisition policy (4-label budget vs controls)
charts.note: The learned scorer beats no acquisition and its shuffled-label control, but loses to simple diverse and random selection at the same budget.

charts.title: Effect of acquisition budget: 1 vs 4 acquired labels per policy
charts.note: Every policy gains from a bigger label budget; diverse selection is best at both budgets, and the learned scorer never leads.

charts.title: Scorer quality is real but unstable across cross-validation folds
charts.note: The utility scorer ranks candidates well above chance in three of four scorable folds but drops below 50% in one, explaining its mixed downstream results.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy_report.md, Fold Diagnostics; fold 0 has no AUC)

charts.title: Hard families decide the outcome: full-task solve rate by family (4-label budget)
charts.x_label: Task family (families with more than one task)
charts.note: The learned scorer's loss to diverse/random comes from Name and Number tasks; it drops Number to 0% while every control stays at 33%+.

charts.title: Shuffled-label control collapses even trivially easy families
charts.note: Shuffling acquired labels zeroes out five families the true-label scorer solves at 100%, confirming the acquired answers carry real signal.

charts.title: Per-row accuracy vs solving all three held-out rows, by policy
charts.note: Roughly a 10-18 point gap separates per-row accuracy from solving all three rows; policy ranking is similar on both metrics.

charts.title: Full-task exact accuracy by selection method
charts.note: The deployable pairwise tournament loses 2.5 points vs direct greedy; the 12.5-point oracle gap is unrealized selection headroom.

charts.title: On headroom tasks the judge just picks candidate A
charts.x_label: Candidate order (normal mode, tasks where greedy is wrong)
charts.note: 0% vs 100% flip with candidate order: pure position bias, not semantic table judging, on the tasks that actually matter.

charts.title: Aggregate direct-vs-correct diagnostic looks fine, misleadingly
charts.note: 80-90% aggregate pick rates are inflated by saturated tasks where both candidates are identical; the headroom subset reveals the failure.

charts.title: Row-level vs full-table exact accuracy by method
charts.note: Every method loses 11-24 points going from per-row accuracy to whole-table exactness; one bad row sinks the table.

charts.title: Where the missed headroom lives: per-task row accuracy, greedy vs oracle pick
charts.x_label: Tasks where the oracle-selected table beats direct greedy
charts.note: All 10 recoverable tasks are DateTime or Number; a better pick exists in the pool (six reach 100%) but the judge never selects it.

charts.title: Pilot (6 tasks) vs main run (40 tasks): full-task exact by method
charts.note: The saturated 6-task pilot hid the story: at 40 tasks the tournament falls below greedy while row repair jumps from 16.7% to 80%.

charts.title: The only table the tournament changed got worse
charts.note: Across 40 tasks the tournament swapped exactly one table, turning a perfect 100% task into 83.3% and losing full-task exactness.

charts.title: Prefix verifier barely closes the gap to answer-verified repair
charts.note: Verifier-guided beam adds only ~1-3pp over greedy; answer-checked repair and the beam oracle show far more headroom.

charts.title: Verifier weight sweep: small gains, then decay
charts.x_label: verifier weight in beam score (0 = logprob only)
charts.note: Moderate verifier weight (1-2) gives the best top-1 accuracy on every split; heavy weighting (8-16) hurts.

charts.title: Correct programs sit in the beam but reranking misses them
charts.note: The beam contains a correct program 73-84% of the time, but top-1 selection lags by 16-29pp — the reranking gap remains.

charts.title: Training set size matters more than any decoder: pilot (128 programs) vs main (512)
charts.note: Quadrupling compiler training data lifts greedy accuracy roughly 5x on most splits, dwarfing every search-time intervention.

charts.title: Verifier learns quickly then plateaus: held-out prefix AUC by training epoch
charts.note: Prefix-state AUC saturates near 0.937 by epoch 5; a strong classifier still fails to steer beam top-1, so AUC is not the bottleneck.

charts.title: Compiler head training: bytecode tokens are learned faster than whole programs
charts.note: By step 600 the compiler gets 66% of opcodes right but only 36% of full programs exact, leaving room search was meant to fill.

charts.title: Held-out composition: program-only compilation beats answer-only supervision
charts.note: Strict program-only stack compilation reaches 72.9% on held-out compositions vs 43.8% for answer-only supervision.

charts.title: Trace + final arm: the procedure is right far more often than the answer token
charts.note: Executing the generated procedure yields 93.8% on held-out compositions while the emitted FINAL line is right only 39.6% of the time.

charts.title: Which task families each supervision style composes
charts.note: Arms have opposite strengths: stack programs ace string and date compositions where answer-style arms score near zero, and vice versa.

charts.title: Template shift flips the ranking: programs overfit surface form
charts.note: Program arms are perfect in-distribution but drop hardest when prompt templates change; answer-style arms degrade far less.

charts.title: Token cost of each output format on composition eval
charts.note: Executable programs cost roughly 3x the tokens of a bare answer, but that budget buys the composition and procedure gains.

charts.title: Learned repair selector vs base compiler and oracle on fresh splits
charts.note: The learned verifier lifts exact execution ~16-19 points over the base compiler but recovers only ~30% of the gap to the oracle.

charts.title: Validation accuracy only improves once training reaches the full repair space
charts.note: The staged curriculum was not the mechanism: accuracy stays at the base rate through the small and medium stages and rises only in the full stage.

charts.title: Fresh paired split: pair reranking beats the single-prompt learned verifier
charts.note: Pair reranking adds accuracy and restores cross-rendering consistency, which the single-prompt learned verifier actually degrades below base.

charts.title: Frozen transformation ABI covers under 20% of public PROSE tasks
charts.note: Even the richest frozen tier validates only 19% of tasks; most misses are missing primitives, not held-out coincidences.

charts.title: Frozen Qwen answers most tasks the ABI cannot cover

series.label: Qwen direct exact match (1 held-out query)

charts.note: The model infers 66% of transformations the frozen ABI misses, so the bottleneck is the ABI's primitives, not the model.

charts.title: Where the ABI fails: coverage by task feature (best tier)
charts.note: Surface substring/casing tasks are reachable; date-time rounding and range arithmetic are completely outside the frozen ABI.

charts.title: ABI coverage by task family (best tier), largest families
charts.note: Semi-structured families like ShippingCode and Email are coverable; the two largest families, DateTime and Number, are nearly uncovered.

charts.title: Why the ABI fails: no train fit dominates, coincidental fits are rare
charts.note: Across all tiers roughly 8 in 10 failures are tasks where no candidate even fits the train rows, not overfit programs failing held-out.

charts.title: Frozen Qwen one-query exact match by task family (60-task sample)
charts.y_label: Exact match on one held-out query
charts.note: Qwen's misses concentrate in DateTime tasks (50% exact) — the same family where the frozen ABI is weakest.

charts.title: Cost of each ABI tier: median candidate programs searched per task
charts.note: Growing the search space ~500x from core to concat lifts coverage only 12.6% to 19.1% — enumeration size is not the bottleneck.

charts.title: Final accuracy by program length: explicit CoT vs silent latent compute (K=8)
charts.note: Silent latent compute never leaves the single-digit floor at any length, while explicit CoT works at length 4 then collapses by length 16.
charts.source: report table (Frozen Probe And CoT Baseline + K Scaling), reports/qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor_report.md

charts.title: K-scaling on held-out length-32 programs: ordered vs shuffled latent positions
charts.note: The shuffled-order control tracks ordered compute within noise (peaking higher at 4.9%), so extra latent positions add capacity, not sequential execution.

charts.title: Held-out operation composition (length 24): more latent compute does not help
charts.note: On unseen operation compositions both arms flatline near 3% at every K, so added latent compute does not recover execution.

charts.title: Seed-to-seed spread on held-out splits (silent ordered, K=8)
charts.note: Training-seed variance (1-8%) is as large as any latent-compute effect, so no single seed's gain is meaningful.

charts.title: Trained silent arms barely clear a zero frozen baseline on held-out splits
charts.note: Silent training adds only 3-5 points over a 0% frozen baseline, and shuffled latent positions match ordered ones everywhere.

charts.title: Token cost per answer: explicit CoT vs silent latent compute
charts.note: CoT emits ~750x more tokens per answer yet still drops to 0% accuracy on lengths 16 and 24.

charts.title: Training loss never converges over 500 steps (all three seeds)
charts.note: Loss stays high and noisy through 500 steps for every seed, so the silent-executor objective was never actually learned.

charts.title: Can a frozen-Qwen reader pick the right repair candidate? (standard split)
charts.note: The best non-oracle selector beats no-repair by only 7pp (51.4% vs 44.4%), capturing 15% of the oracle gap — reading works, but weakly.

charts.title: More readable context hurts: what the frozen reader sees vs accuracy
charts.x_label: information given to the frozen reader
charts.note: Adding claimed values and execution traces makes the frozen reader worse, collapsing from 50% to near 0% — extra text distracts, not informs.

charts.title: Gated trained selector vs baseline and oracle across evaluation splits
charts.note: The gated selector improves or matches base on every split without damaging correct programs, but stays far below the shortlist oracle.

charts.title: Training loss: real labels learn, shuffled labels never do
charts.note: Loss drops steadily with real labels but stays flat near 3.7 when labels are shuffled: the head is learning genuine candidate signal.

charts.title: Which generator seed you test on matters more than the selector
charts.note: Per-seed spread is huge: base accuracy runs 0% to 92% across generator seeds, so headroom for repair is concentrated in seed 123.

charts.title: Gating buys safety: damage collapses while recovery mostly survives
charts.note: Validation-tuned gating cuts damage from 94% to 22% (and 9% to 0% for the trained head) while the trained head keeps its 12.5% recovery.

charts.title: Shuffled-label control: the trained head uses real signal, not artifacts
charts.note: Training on shuffled labels drops accuracy far below the no-repair baseline on both evaluations, confirming the learned signal is real.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen_readable_candidate_verifier_report.md, Standard L24 Gate and Held-Out Source Readout, seed 101)

charts.title: Held-out task coverage by ABI variant and search depth
charts.note: The full office+table ABI at depth 2 covers 84% of 38 real-style tasks; the minimal core ABI covers only 21%.

charts.title: Coverage by task split under the primary ABI (office+table, depth 2)
charts.note: Held-out compositions and new families are fully covered, but every deliberately out-of-ABI stress task fails.

charts.title: Outcome breakdown under the primary gate (office+table, depth 2)
charts.note: Failures split into overfitting to visible examples (5%) and tasks the ABI/search cannot even fit (11%).

charts.title: Learned recurrent repair vs its ceilings, per evaluation split
charts.note: The learned repair loop lifts base accuracy 2-3x on every split, but oracle and supervised ceilings show most headroom remains.

charts.title: DAgger rounds fix the policy's own mistakes
charts.note: On-policy correction raises rollout success 32% to 59% and argument-edit accuracy 27% to 82% over three DAgger rounds.

charts.title: Held-out task success by method: gated program induction beats direct answering
charts.note: Raw induced programs underperform direct answering (40% vs 56%), but gating on non-memorizing train-passing programs lifts it to 64%.

charts.title: Tasks helped vs hurt relative to the direct row-by-row baseline
charts.note: Ungated programs win 2 tasks but break 6-7 that direct answering solved; the gate keeps both wins while introducing zero losses.

charts.title: Full-task exact by task family: where programs help and where they break
charts.note: Program induction genuinely helps Number tasks but wrecks DateTime, FilePath and BillingCode; the gate matches the best arm per family.

charts.title: Row-level accuracy vs strict full-task exactness by method
charts.note: Direct answering loses 25pts moving from row to full-task exactness; program arms lose little because they fail whole tasks at once.

charts.title: Passing visible examples does not mean generalizing to held-out rows
charts.note: Raw programs pass visible examples on 52% of tasks but solve only 40% held-out; the shuffled control even train-passes 20% yet solves 4%.

charts.title: Row-level accuracy by task family: where induced programs collapse
charts.note: Programs match direct answering on Currency/Email/Phone/Rating but collapse on DateTime, BillingCode, and FilePath even at the row level.

charts.title: Frozen Qwen: locking in a rule does not beat answering rows directly
charts.note: Committing to one reusable rule loses 10pp of row accuracy vs direct answering; shuffled rules drop further, so rules carry signal but hurt.

charts.title: Static executable decomposition: recursion lifts the oracle, not example selection
charts.note: Recursive trees raise the held-out oracle from 29.4% to 34.3%, but example-selected recursion ties monolithic at 22.7%; the shuffled control collapses to 0.3%.

charts.title: Train-verifying rules did not recover the lost row accuracy
charts.note: Generating three rule styles and picking the one that best fits the training examples still leaves locked-rule row accuracy below direct answering.

charts.title: Where recursive decomposition lifts the static oracle, by task family
charts.note: Recursion's oracle gain is concentrated in delimiter-structured families (Log, Url, Language, Address); DateTime and Number barely move.

charts.title: Static selection gap: oracle vs train-example-selected recursive programs, by family
charts.note: Train-example selection loses the whole recursive gain in Address and City and a large share in Name, Phone, and BillingCode; only Email keeps oracle-level coverage.

charts.title: Per-task effect of locking a rule: helps 2 tasks, damages 8
charts.x_label: Task (only tasks where locking changed row accuracy)
charts.note: Rule locking rescues two DateTime tasks but collapses Number tasks Qwen solved directly (100% to 16.7%, 83.3% to 0%).

charts.title: Train verification is a blind filter: every rule fits training yet fails held-out rows
charts.note: All 30 selected rules scored 1.0 on training rows, so train verification cannot distinguish rules that will fail on held-out rows.

charts.title: Length-24 execution: paired-consistency training vs matched control
charts.note: Paired-consistency training lifts length-24 execution over a matched control, but long prompt-invariant execution stays unsolved.

charts.title: Executor accuracy vs program length (main run)
charts.note: The trained length range (4-12) is solved essentially perfectly; accuracy collapses at length 24 on every split.

charts.title: Where length-24 fails: per-slot accuracy on standard L24 (main run)
charts.note: Individual slots are mostly right (80-94%), but small per-op errors compound over 24 steps, so exact execution drops to 25%.

charts.title: Register-token compiler accuracy vs chain length
charts.note: The trace-supervised register interface works for chains up to length 12 (~89-95%) but collapses at held-out length 24.

charts.title: Length-24 accuracy: register trace vs control heads
charts.note: Even where length-24 is hard, the trace-supervised register run beats both controls, which stay at chance (1/97 is about 1%).

charts.title: Where length-24 fails: per-component vs whole-program accuracy
charts.note: At length 24 each program slot is still ~89-97% correct, but 24 slots must all be right, so exact-program and executor accuracy collapse.

charts.title: Training curve: executor accuracy by chain length across curriculum stages
charts.note: Accuracy stays near zero for 300 steps, then short/medium lengths jump together in the third stage while length 24 lags far behind.

charts.title: Design iterations: only the final LoRA + trace recipe lifts length-24 accuracy
charts.note: Five pilot configurations all stayed at 0-3% on standard length-24; only the final 600-step curriculum run reached 21.9%.

charts.title: Paired-prompt consistency: same-program agreement collapses at length 24
charts.note: Two phrasings of the same task compile to matching register states 98-99% of the time through length 12, but only 3.1% at length 24.

charts.title: Executor accuracy on fresh length-24 programs: base compiler vs learned refiner vs oracle selection
charts.note: The learned refiner recovers only a small slice of the oracle gap (+3.1pp on standard, nothing on paraphrase).

charts.title: How often the correct program even exists in the local repair set
charts.note: The key diagnostic: local two-edit repair rarely contains the correct program, worst on paraphrases (7%), capping any selector.

charts.title: Share of the base-to-oracle gap the learned refiner recovers
charts.note: Even where good candidates exist, the verifier picks them only sometimes: about 23% of the gap on standard, 0% on paraphrase.

charts.title: Active VM-controller accuracy by split: search distillation did not beat behavior cloning
charts.note: Search distillation helped only val mixed (6.2% to 12.5%); the oracle gap (69-100%) shows large headroom the recipe failed to capture.

charts.title: Accuracy vs edit budget K for the search-distilled policy (greedy learned mode)
charts.note: No clean monotonic K-scaling: more VM edits help only fresh paraphrase, while other splits stay flat or degrade from the K=0 blank baseline.

charts.title: Local training accuracy by phase and epoch: search labels lowered action accuracy
charts.note: Retraining on search-repaired labels dropped action accuracy from 60.0% to 50.4%, and local gains never translated into better active rollouts.

charts.title: No-answer beam guidance: exact-prefix value helps a little, semantic value does not
charts.note: Exact-prefix value gives the only top-1 gain (+2.3pp on fresh paired); semantic value never beats plain logprob beam search.
charts.source: report table in reports/qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model_paper.md (best-weight rows per decoder family)

charts.title: The correct program is usually in the beam; top-1 ranking loses it
charts.note: Beam oracle sits 14-20pp above top-1 selection: the gap is ranking/calibration, not candidate generation.
charts.source: report table in reports/qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model_paper.md (Candidate Oracle Gap section)

charts.title: Label density in the main run: semantic reachability is a much broader target
charts.note: Raw reachability marks most actions positive; top-1 filtering tightens it, but semantic positives stay 4-5x denser than exact ones.
charts.source: report table in reports/qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model_paper.md (Label Density, main_semantic_prefix_value_s512_top1 row)

charts.title: Value model quality: both labels are learnable, exact is cleaner
charts.note: Both value models learn their target well above chance, yet even 0.94 AUC exact supervision converts to only a small top-1 gain.

charts.title: Pilot iteration tightened the semantic label
charts.note: Successive rank filtering cut semantic positives from 69.4% to 35.2% of actions, while exact positives stayed under 8%.

charts.title: Exact answer accuracy vs program length (standard wording)
charts.note: Only trace supervision installs an executable latent compiler; it works through length 12 but collapses at length 24.

charts.title: Trace parser: trained wording vs paraphrased wording
charts.note: The learned parser is format-sensitive: rewording the same programs drops execution to near chance at every length.

charts.title: Answer-only continuation erodes the installed parser (standard wording)
charts.note: Final-answer-only training damages the trace-installed interface; low learning rate preserves length 4 but still loses longer programs.

charts.title: Why execution collapses: per-step parse accuracy vs length (trace parser)
charts.note: Small per-step argument errors compound: every symbol must be right, so ~94% per-step accuracy already halves exact execution by length 12.

charts.title: Architecture ablation: which parser designs execute longer programs
charts.note: Deeper read layer and the after-operation argument reader each extend executable program length; layer-4 probes die past 4 ops.

charts.title: Probability mass on the correct answer collapses to chance past 4 ops
charts.note: Beyond 4 operations the executor's mass on the true answer matches the direct baseline's ~1% chance level: failure is total, not partial.

charts.title: Slot-repair editor vs frozen compiler vs local-repair oracle
charts.note: The editor beats the frozen base only on validation; on fresh prompts it matches or hurts, far below the ~85% oracle ceiling.

charts.title: Share of the base-to-oracle gap the editor recovers, by split
charts.note: Positive gap recovery on train/validation flips negative on every fresh distribution - the learned repair signal does not transfer.

charts.title: Editor validation accuracy across training epochs
charts.note: The editor only overtakes the frozen base at the final epoch, and the curve is noisy - the validation gain is marginal, not a stable capability.

charts.title: Editing breaks paraphrase consistency on paired prompts
charts.note: The editor nearly halves cross-paraphrase answer agreement (69.5% to 37.9%), while oracle repairs raise it to 88.3%.

charts.title: Pilot ablations: no editor variant wins on both validation and fresh prompts
charts.note: The 512-example main run is best on validation (35.2%) but pilots like value-stabilized beat it on fresh prompts (35.9% vs 27.7%).

charts.title: Edit-gate precision never stabilizes during training
charts.note: Which-slot-to-edit precision oscillates between 45% and 71% across 18 epochs, so the sparse edit decision never becomes reliable.

charts.title: Exact execution accuracy by chain length (standard prompts)
charts.note: Trace-supervised compilers solve short and medium chains far above chance; answer-only QLoRA stays at chance at every length.

charts.title: Exact execution accuracy on paraphrased prompts
charts.note: The stability loss's clearest win is paraphrase length 24 (23.4% vs 15.6%); at other lengths the two compiler arms are essentially tied.

charts.title: Does the stability loss make paired answers more consistent?
charts.note: The explicit stability penalty is a mixed regularizer: it helps at length 4 and 24 but loses badly at length 8 and ties at 12.

charts.title: Paired length-12: stability's mid-training edge disappears by step 600
charts.note: At step 300 the stability arm led on paired L12 (35.9% vs 4.7% consistency), but the matched control fully catches up by step 600.
charts.source: report table (reports/qwen_slot_stability_compiler_paper.md, Training Dynamics + Exact Execution tables)

charts.title: Where compilation breaks: per-slot decode accuracy vs exact program match
charts.note: Init and op slots stay near-perfect; small per-argument errors compound so exact 24-step programs fall to zero (stability run, standard prompts).

charts.title: Paraphrase accuracy by checkpoint: the second half of training does the heavy lifting
charts.note: At step 300 the stability loss protects length-12 paraphrases (0.25 vs 0.05), but by step 600 the plain paired compiler catches up.

charts.title: Exact execution on trained length-4 programs: trace bootstrap vs controls
charts.note: Attention-aligned trace bootstrap reaches 99.6% exact execution; direct and answer-only controls stay at chance (~1%).

charts.title: Execution accuracy vs program length: no transfer beyond trained slots
charts.note: The installed interface is length-specific: near-perfect at the trained length 4, chance-level at untrained lengths 8-24.

charts.title: Trained wording vs paraphrase at length 4: symbol extraction breaks down
charts.note: Paraphrasing mostly preserves initial-value extraction but halves op/arg accuracy, collapsing exact execution to 1.6%.

charts.title: Stronger length-12 training: high per-step accuracy still compounds to failure
charts.note: Per-step symbol accuracy near 90% at L=12 still yields under 6% exact execution: a compounding-error regime.

charts.title: Readout architecture ablation: only attention readouts bootstrap execution
charts.note: Pooled readouts stay at chance (~1%); adding attention-aligned queries jumps trace-trained execution to 99% at the trained length.

charts.title: Length-12 curriculum grid: no schedule escapes the length cliff
charts.note: Every length-12 curriculum still collapses by 12 steps; fixed and mixed schedules also give up most length-4 accuracy.

charts.title: Curriculum trade-off: mixed-length training buys paraphrase robustness, costs accuracy
charts.note: Mixed and fixed curricula keep most accuracy under paraphrase, while high-accuracy standard curricula drop from 96-99% to near chance.

charts.title: Training curve: trace bootstrap saturates by step 500, paraphrase never moves
charts.note: The compiler locks onto trained wording within 500 steps and stays at 99%, while paraphrase accuracy stays flat at chance throughout.

charts.title: Length-24 exact execution: curriculum drives gains, state ladder does not beat control
charts.note: The staged length curriculum lifts long-chain execution far above the answer-only baseline; adding state-ladder loss never beats the matched control.

charts.title: Exact execution vs program length on paired splits (final checkpoints)
charts.note: All compiler arms match at short lengths; the full-weight state ladder degrades fastest as chains get longer, especially at length 24.

charts.title: Best logged paired length-24 checkpoint (step 800)
charts.note: By best-logged checkpoint the lighter w=0.25 state loss wins (28.1%), but its final saved checkpoint still trails the no-state control.

charts.title: Paired length-24 state consistency collapses under full-weight state loss
charts.note: At the final checkpoint the full-weight state ladder nearly destroys paired length-24 consistency; the no-state control keeps the highest.

charts.title: State-ladder edge at 400 steps disappears by 900 steps
charts.note: The state ladder leads the control at a 400-step budget but falls behind it once training runs to 900 steps.

charts.title: Full-weight state loss wipes out paraphrase generalization at length 24
charts.note: All runs drop on paraphrased prompts, but the full-weight state ladder collapses to 0% while lighter or no state loss keeps ~14-16%.

charts.title: Long-chain execution only emerges in the final curriculum stage

charts.title: Per-token accuracy stays high while whole programs fail at length 24
charts.note: Init and op tokens are near-perfect; scattered argument errors (87-93% per token) compound over 24 ops to sink whole-program exactness.

charts.title: Final length-24 accuracy by training arm
charts.note: The max-24 curriculum compiler dominates on standard and paired splits; copied structural expansion only helps on held-out wording.

charts.title: Accuracy vs program length at the final checkpoint (standard split)

charts.title: Length-24 accuracy: standard wording vs seen-family paraphrases
charts.note: expand_copy inverts: 75% on paraphrases but 1.6% on standard wording, so template wording, not just arm, drives length-24 failures.

charts.title: Paired-prompt consistency at length 24: only the curriculum arm holds up

charts.title: Executable accuracy after expansion to 24 slots, by program length
charts.note: The final 24-slot compiler keeps perfect accuracy on length-8 and length-16 programs and reaches 82.8-100% on length-24 splits.

charts.title: Accuracy across expansion stages (standard split)
charts.note: Expanding from 8 to 16 to 24 slots never degrades shorter lengths; length 16 starts at 98.4% and reaches 100%, length 24 ends at 82.8%.

charts.title: Paraphrase-pair consistency of the final compiler
charts.note: Paraphrased prompts compile to the same latent execution trace 87.5% of the time even at length 24, with near-perfect state prefixes.

charts.title: Frozen Qwen features drive a latent program executor only with trace supervision
charts.note: Trace-supervised compilation hits 96-100% while a direct classifier and an answer-only compiler on the same frozen features stay at 97-way chance.

charts.title: Length generalization: trained on 1-4 steps, evaluated out to 24

charts.title: Repair success: only trace-trained model with trace at inference repairs the code
charts.note: Every control sits at 0% while trace-conditioned SFT evaluated with the trace repairs 60/60 on both splits.

charts.title: Ablation: the same trace-trained model fails without a correct trace
charts.note: Removing or shuffling the trace at inference drops the trace-SFT model from 100% to 0% — the trace content, not the format, carries the answer.

charts.title: Copying the expected token from the trace
charts.note: The frozen base copies the right token from the trace only 18% (IID) / 3% (holdout) of the time; trace SFT makes copying reliable at 100%.

charts.title: Edit localization vs actual repair (IID validation)
charts.note: Every fine-tuned variant deletes the wrong token, but only the trace-trained model with a correct trace writes the right replacement.

charts.title: Format compliance is free with SFT; correctness is not
charts.note: All SFT variants emit well-formed, syntactically valid patches; only the trace-conditioned model produces patches that pass tests.

charts.title: Pilot generation-budget sweep: 64 tokens is too few
charts.x_label: Max new tokens (pilot trace adapter + trace)
charts.note: With a 64-token cap the trace-trained adapter cannot even finish an applicable patch; 128 tokens flips repair from 0 to 1.

charts.title: Transcription is a model-level LAW: ≈1.0 in every family
charts.note: Plan-given execution is near-perfect across three unrelated substrates — the fixed 4B is a universal reliable compiler.

charts.title: Simulation is SUBSTRATE-DEPENDENT (C15's decay constant was list-specific)
charts.note: Register (compact 3-int state) simulates robustly; list decays; a mutating char string is floored — simulation fidelity is set by the state representation, not depth.

charts.title: The generation wall replicates: identification collapses in every family
charts.note: Identifying a novel composition collapses toward chance with depth everywhere — 'the model executes what it cannot invent' is model-level.

charts.title: Selection is FREE: every selector reaches the coverage ceiling (list)
charts.note: Whenever the correct program is sampled, an 8-example execute-filter, the model's own verifier, and even a random pick among consistent candidates all recover it — selection loss is 0. The wall is coverage, not selection.

charts.title: Same on the register machine — selectors = coverage ceiling
charts.note: Single-shot undersells 2–5×; sample+filter recovers it — but that IS sample-more.

charts.title: Register coverage@k: the wall's depth is set by hypothesis-space size
charts.note: Register stays sample-accessible deeper than list (smaller 12-op menu → sampled more often), mechanistically explaining C16's register identification floor as coverage-driven.

charts.title: Banking: CONCENTRATION at depth 1, EXPANSION at depth 2
charts.note: Depth 1: banking pulls coverage into the single-shot (0.60→0.80), ceiling flat = concentration. Depth 2: the coverage ceiling itself rises 0.15→0.45 (3×) on held-out tasks = expansion, proposing compositions the base never sampled.

charts.title: Depth-2 EXPANSION: banking triples the coverage ceiling (held-out)
charts.note: The banked model proposes correct depth-2 compositions the base never sampled — unique-program count even drops, so banking moved the proposal mass onto correct compositions (C17's lever).

charts.title: Context composes the module (0.95) but format capture crushes deployable 2AFC (0.51)
charts.note: An explicit simulate-both-compare procedure lifts base 2AFC to 0.83. The weight-installed SIM module reaches 0.95 parse-conditional (it genuinely composes in-context) but format capture (parse 0.53) hijacks the output channel half the time, crushing deployable accuracy to 0.51 — capability = module x interface x procedure.

charts.title: No context strategy moves hypothesis generation (the un-composable wall)
charts.note: Neither a procedure, worked demos, nor a working simulator moves generation — proposal cannot be assembled in-context from forward primitives.

charts.title: VM-agent loop works (10%->43%), but ECHO does not help; the oracle gap remains
charts.note: The recurrent model-VM edit loop lifts a 10% blank baseline to 43.1% average and beats native Qwen by 6.2%. ECHO (also training on VM observation tokens) reached 36.2% (-6.9% vs action-only) despite far lower observation CE — modeling the textual VM state does not optimize the action policy. The oracle teacher (73.8%) marks the remaining policy gap.

charts.title: Representation >> expression at shallow depth; both thin at the wall
charts.note: The composition's first operation is linearly decoded from residual-stream activations at 0.99 at depth 1 while the model names it only 0.44 and generates it 0.68 = latent capability (present but unexpressed). The gap decays with depth: at depth 3 (the wall) the probe (0.27) barely clears the shuffled-label overfitting floor (0.14), so the real signal ~0.13 equals the model's behavioral naming -- the representation itself has thinned to a thread. The wall is an expression failure when shallow, a representation failure when deep.

charts.title: Where the first op is computed: layer profile of the probe
charts.note: Depth-1 first-op rises from chance at the embedding to ~0.99 by layer 15 and plateaus (computed in early-mid layers). Deeper compositions are represented progressively less: depth 2 plateaus ~0.4, depth 3 hovers ~0.2 -- above the 0.10 chance line but a thread. The inverse-inference the model needs to propose is computed for shallow structure and largely not computed for deep structure.

charts.title: Steering the decodable first-op direction does NOT move behavior (best coef)
charts.note: C19 showed the first op is linearly DECODABLE (depth-1 probe 0.99). Adding that same mean-difference direction back to the residual stream during generation does NOT make the model use it: at depth 1 steering toward the true op (0.36) is no better than baseline (0.33) or random (0.35); at depth 2 a faint whiff (0.17 vs 0.12) is within noise of the random control and below the pre-registered +0.10 bar. Decodability != steerability -- the latent signal is readable but not writable into behavior. Only weight edits (banking C18) and tools (C12) move the wall.

charts.title: Depth-2 naming vs steering coefficient (all conditions flat/overlapping)
charts.note: steer_true sits marginally above steer_random and steer_wrong at low coef (a directional whiff) but the gap is within noise; beyond coef ~12 fluency degrades and all conditions fall. No usable steering effect. Baseline (no steer) = 0.12.

charts.title: Banking depth-1+2 installs depth-2 (3x) but unlocks ZERO depth-3 coverage
charts.note: Apex bootstrapping test: bank ONLY depth-1+2 self-solutions (83 depth-2 pairs, no depth-3 examples), does the banked model now sample depth-3? Depth 2 install works and generalizes to held-out tasks (0.12->0.36, tripled). But depth 3 stays at exactly 0.00 -- a strong depth-2 composition skill does NOT length-generalize up. DEPTH-LOCAL: self-banking installs only depths already samplable; it cannot climb the wall. To extend the frontier you must SEED each rung externally with tool-search (C12), then bank.

charts.title: Depth-3 coverage@k: base and banked1 both pinned to zero (no unlock)
charts.note: The banked model's depth-2 coverage climbs to 0.36 with more samples, but its depth-3 coverage is flat at 0.00 -- identical to base. Banking a depth-2 skill buys nothing at depth 3.

charts.title: Tool-seeded banking crosses depth-3 (0->0.125) where self-banking gave 0 -- but weakly
charts.note: C21: self-banking depth-1+2 gave depth-3 coverage exactly 0.00 (base samples ~0 depth-3 to bank). Here we harvest depth-3 via an interpreter-backed explorer (CPU, no external model, 130/130 solved), add to the SAME depth-1+2 pairs, and bank. Depth-3 think coverage crosses 0.00->0.125 (5/40 distinct novel held-out tasks, significant vs the 0/40 floor) -- validating the recipe: tools explore, banking installs. BUT the install is weak and test-time-dominated: depth-3 no-think greedy@1 stays 0.00, vs depth-2 which installs deployably (greedy@1 0.15). The installer's efficacy decays with depth (echoing C19: depth-3 representation is a thread).

charts.title: Depth-3 coverage@k (think): banked_tool lifts off zero; base stays pinned
charts.note: The banked model's depth-3 think-coverage climbs to 0.125 with more samples while base is flat at 0.00. Depth-2 (contrast) climbs to 0.42. The crossing is real but modest, and it is test-time think-search coverage; deployable no-think single-shot barely moves at depth 3.

charts.title: Depth-3 install is DATA-LIMITED: coverage rises with #tool-found pairs (no plateau)
charts.note: C22 crossed the depth-3 wall but weakly (0.125) and test-time-only, leaving open: data-limited or representational cap? Bank N tool-found depth-3 solutions (interpreter search over the substrate's own DSL, no external model). Depth-3 think coverage rises MONOTONICALLY 0.00->0.087->0.212->0.375 across N=0/40/160/640 with NON-OVERLAPPING Wilson CIs (top-dose lower CI 0.28 > low-dose upper CI 0.17) -- DATA-LIMITED, not a cap. Crucially the DEPLOYABLE single-shot install scales too: no-think greedy@1 0.00->0.10 at N=640 (~0 at C22's N=130). 0-leakage held-out set (novel depth-3 rules by function AND op-composition) => genuine generalization. The deep wall is a DATA bottleneck, not a hard representational cap: tool-seeded banking scales with data into deployable capability.

charts.title: Depth-3 coverage@k fans out with dose (base flat at 0)
charts.note: Each dose's coverage@k curve sits progressively higher: base pinned at 0, N=40 to 0.09, N=160 to 0.21, N=640 to 0.375. More tool-found depth-3 solutions -> more installed depth-3 capability on novel held-out rules, with no sign of saturation through N=640.
